input: topic: We just released a new climate emulator to explore the application of Stratospheric Aerosol Injection (SAI) to mitigate global warming!
output:
We just released a new climate emulator to explore the application of Stratospheric Aerosol Injection (SAI) to mitigate global warming! 

SAI uses reflective particles in the atmosphere to reflect sunlight and thereby cool Earth’s surface. Our emulator lets you explore how different ways to apply SAI might affect average global temperature. 

Please check out the emulator at http://planetparasol.ai. 

SAI is a promising direction, but we still need more research to better understand its impact and potential implementation. 

Big thanks to collaborators Jeremy Irvin, Daniele Visioni, Ben Kravitz, Dakota Gruener, Chris Smith and Duncan Watson-Parris.

input: topic: Please consider signing this letter as I did.
output:
Please consider signing this letter as I did.
SB1047 is a California bill that attempts to regulate AI research and development, creating obstacles to the dissemination open research in AI and open source AI platforms.
Regulating the deployment of AI applications is fine.
But regulating R&D would have apocalyptic consequences on the AI ecosystem.
The sad thing is that the regulation of AI R&D is predicated on the illusion of "existential risks" pushed by a handful of delusional think-tanks, and dismissed as nonsense (or at least widely premature) by the vast majority of researchers and engineers in academia, startups, larger companies, and investment firms.

https://stopsb1047.com/

input: topic: Lisa Ling opened our panel at Dell Technologies asking us to comment on Elon Musk’s prediction at the Milken Institute that AI will overtake humans to the point that 'biological intelligence will be 1%’. | tags: #DellTechWorld #AI #HumanFirst
output:
Lisa Ling opened our panel at Dell Technologies asking us to comment on Elon Musk’s prediction at the Milken Institute that AI will overtake humans to the point that 'biological intelligence will be 1%’. I said “If I may, I am going to disagree with Elon” (to which the ~2000 audience members laughed :).

Loved being part of this thought provoking and intimate conversation in front of thousands at #DellTechWorld for the “AI: The Accelerator of Our Generation” panel with John Roese, Dr. Paul Calleja and and moderated by the awesome Lisa Ling. 

I believe that we need both biological and artificial intelligence. Biological intelligence will evolve to leverage AI and if we build AI in service of humanity, then sure, biological intelligence may only be 1% but it will be SO POWERFUL. 

We also discussed how AI can help with access and equity, especially in education, healthcare and sustainable living, and what industries are set to benefit the most from using AI (answer: every industry!!) And I shared my experience with my AI clone and the opportunities this opens up. 

I love that we also discussed how to best equip our kids for an #AI driven future. As parents, grandparents, aunts, uncles, etc how do we raise young people in the age of AI? I have lots of thoughts on this, but my high level answer is that we must double down on skills such as empathy, creativity, innovative, collaboration, critical thinking and of course authentic human connection. We must put humans at the center! #HumanFirst

input: topic: Something all my friends know about me is that theatre (re!) is one of the great loves of my life.
output:
Something all my friends know about me is that theatre (re!) is one of the great loves of my life.

When I was 6, I got to be a "snake" in my town's local production of The Lion King (there were no snakes). A couple of years later, when my dad acted in a play, I got to sit back stage and marvel at the magic of it: a set could be anywhere and (with a bit of talent) you could be anyone. And those lights! 

That love of theatre grew with me, from taking classes in it to turning my keynotes into performances. The AI expert in me writes the script, but it's the thespian in me that works hard to breathe life into it (and into the audience... like the one that made the effort to come see me open the Generative AI Summit at 8:30 AM today in London - sorry it was so early, guys).

I've been asked whether I find it odd to love something so human (theatre), yet spend most of my time working on AI.

Well...



IT'S ALL HUMAN! (!!!)

If you haven't yet learned to see the human behind the AI curtain, here's a blog post for you: https://lnkd.in/e6Uckj8a

For those waiting for AGI to knock on your door and tell you to hand over the keys... one of the best skills you can develop in the AI space is spotting the human parts of the system. Those are weak points where aspirations of usefulness, objectivity, and effectiveness circle the drain, warning us to account for human elements when we design teach. Ignoring (or, worse, actively disbelieving) that AI is a way to amplify a few humans to the benefit or detriment of many is a recipe for disaster. Let's keep an eye on those humans and make sure they have the tools and help they need to create a positive impact rather than a slew of unintended whoopsies.

Technology is a tool for increasing productivity and solving previously unsolvable problems. We're tool-making apes and always have been. AI is incredible, but it's not an either/or between advancing our humanity and advancing our tools. And it's not an either or between art and tech. Plays—and the arts in general—are a beautiful place for weaving in tech to tell old stories in new ways.

I had the treat of seeing Dorian Gray recently. Bravo to both Sarah Snook's performance and the use of tech. Screens suspended above the stage showed live video being captured by camera operators on stage, while Snook changed roles and costumes (and beards) without missing a stride. From the book, you'll remember that Dorian is out of control towards the end, beautiful but depraved -- Snook whips out an iPhone and the screen above changes to selfie POV, as Snook slides the face filter up hideously to a fat-lipped bug-eyed Dorian in makeup maximum while hitting the script's crescendo. The audience was in awe. Trust me, that moment was everything theatre aspires to be.

If you're working in tech, I hope you're finding time to make or take in art of some kind. It will make our tech better.

The photo below is from Captain Amazing. If you're in London, see it. It was amazing!

input:
topic: “The path to AGI looks a lot more like curing cancer than developing a vaccine” - Alexandr Wang, Scale AI CEO

I am very much an AI optimist.
output:
“The path to AGI looks a lot more like curing cancer than developing a vaccine” - Alexandr Wang, Scale AI CEO

I am very much an AI optimist. But I don't spend much time thinking about "Is AI smarter than us?" or "when will we have AGI?"

I do, however, spend a great deal of time thinking about: 

1) How current AI capabilities can solve new problems?

and 

2) How we build capabilities to steer and harness complex technology?

Bloomberg reported yesterday that OpenAI has come up with a set of five levels to track its progress towards building AGI (link here: https://lnkd.in/e5Zg6MKM)


- Level 1: Chatbots, AI with conversational language

- Level 2: Reasoners, human-level problem solving

- Level 3: Agents, systems that can take actions

- Level 4: Innovators, AI that can aid in invention

- Level 5: Organizations, AI that can do the work of an organization

I'm curious to hear more from OpenAI, but I have doubts that AI progress will ever fit into such a linear box, especially when none of those fuzzy terms like "aid in invention"* are properly defined (or at least properly defined for and by those who will use them most).

I more closely resonate with Alexandr's belief that the path to AGI looks more like curing cancer than building a vaccine: solving new problems, running into roadblocks, finding a way to unblock the roadblocks, running into new challenges, rinse and repeat.

We are a tool building species that has been solving problems through automation since the dawn of time. 

Focusing on what we want to solve rather than how fast we make something "smarter" (whatever that means*) than us feels a much more exciting and worthy goal.

You can hear more from Alexandr in the clip below: https://lnkd.in/efwJUtMY

*Pens aid in invention already, as do random number generators and AI-based grammar checkers. "Inventors." Pfft. There's always a human in there, they're just getting scaled bigger and bigger while the outside observer struggles to see their role in the system.
**A person with a great tool for their needs is smarter than that same person without the tool. And that's all AI is: a tool. The trick is in understanding whose tool and at what scale.

input: topic: As a newly minted professor in the Stanford University Department of Computer Science in 2009, Dr. | tags: #AskMoreOfAI #deeplearning #imagenet #alexnet
output:
As a newly minted professor in the Stanford University Department of Computer Science in 2009, Dr. Fei-Fei Li was inspired both by math and by observing how humans learn to try a new, large-scale data-driven approach to training AI models. She created ImageNet, an Internet-scale data set containing 12 million images along with an international competition to see who could build the best model to classify these images. 

At the time, many researchers dismissed ImageNet as being too large and complex. But then in 2012, a University of Toronto student named Alex Krizhevsky proved them wrong with his winning submission AlexNet, which ran on just two NVIDIA GPUs and trounced the next best competitor by a whopping 10.8% margin. Today, this practice of training massive models on GPUs with Internet-scale data has given rise to large language models like ChatGPT and a new era of computing.

I loved chatting with Dr. Li for this week’s episode of my #AskMoreOfAI podcast -- listen or watch here👇🏼 to hear her incredible story about conviction and persistence in the face of naysayers and how she sees AI revolutionizing healthcare, robotics, education, and every aspect of humanity. 
https://lnkd.in/gFEh9R8f

#deeplearning #imagenet #alexnet I really enjoyed this conversation, Clara Shih - thank you!

input: topic: New short course on Pretraining LLMs!
output:
New short course on Pretraining LLMs! Developed with Upstage and taught by their CEO Sung Kim and CSO Lucy Park

While prompting or fine-tuning existing models works well for many general language tasks, pretraining is  valuable for specialized domains or languages with limited representation in current models.

This course walks you through the LLM pretraining pipeline:
1. Data preparation: Learn to source, clean, and prepare training data using HuggingFace.
2. Model architecture: Configure transformer networks, including modifying existing models.
3. Training: Set up and run training using open-source libraries.
4. Evaluation: Benchmark performance using popular evaluation strategies.

As an example use case, you'll also compare the output of a base model with its fine-tuned and further pretrained variants, to see the impact of pretraining on a model's ability to write Python. 

The course also explores an innovative technique called depth up-scaling, which Upstage used to train their Solar model family, reducing pretraining compute costs by up to 70%. This technique works by first duplicating layers of a smaller pretrained model to form a larger model, and then further pretraining the result.

Sign up here! https://lnkd.in/g4k98UCr

input: topic: A barrier to faster progress in generative AI is evaluations (evals), particularly of custom AI  applications that generate free-form text.
output:
A barrier to faster progress in generative AI is evaluations (evals), particularly of custom AI  applications that generate free-form text. Let’s say you have a multi-agent research system that includes a researcher agent and a writer agent. Would adding a fact-checking agent improve the results? If we can’t efficiently evaluate the impact of such changes, it’s hard to know which changes to keep.

For evaluating general-purpose foundation models such as large language models (LLMs) — which are trained to respond to a large variety of prompts — we have standardized tests like MMLU (multiple-choice questions) and HumanEval (testing code generation); the LMSYS Chatbot arena, which pits two LLMs’ responses against each other and asks a human to judge which response is superior; and large-scale benchmarking like HELM. These evaluation tools are invaluable for giving LLM users a sense of different models' relative performance. Nonetheless, they have limitations: For example, leakage of benchmarks datasets’ questions and answers into training data is a constant worry, and human preference for certain answers does not mean those answers are more accurate.

In contrast, our current options for evaluating specific applications built using LLMs are far more limited. Here, I see two major types of applications.
- For applications designed to deliver unambiguous, right-or-wrong responses, we have reasonable options. Let’s say we want an LLM to read a resume and extract the candidate's most recent job title, or read a customer email and route it to the right department. We can create a test set that comprises ground-truth labeled examples with the right responses, and measure the percentage of times the LLM generates the right output. The main bottleneck is creating the labeled test set, which is expensive but surmountable.
- But many LLM-based applications generate free-text output with no single right response. For example, if we ask an LLM to summarize customer emails, there’s a multitude of possible good (and bad) responses. The same holds for a system to do web research and write an article about a topic, or a RAG system for answering questions. It’s impractical to hire an army of human experts to read the LLM’s outputs every time we tweak the algorithm and evaluate if the answers have improved — we need an automated way to test the outputs. Thus, many teams use an advanced language model to evaluate outputs. In the customer email summarization example, we might design an evaluation rubric (scoring criteria) for what makes a good summary. Given an email summary generated by our system, we might prompt an advanced LLM to read it and score it according to our rubric. I’ve found that the results of such a procedure, while better than nothing, can also be noisy — sometimes too noisy to reliably tell me if the way I’ve tweaked an algorithm is good or bad.

input: topic: Starting today, open source is leading the way.
output:
Starting today, open source is leading the way. Introducing Llama 3.1: Our most capable models yet.

Today we’re releasing a collection of new models including our long awaited 405B. Llama 3.1 delivers stronger reasoning, a larger 128K context window & improved support for 8 languages including English — among other improvements.

Details in the full announcement ➡️ https://go.fb.me/hvuqhb
Download the models ➡️ https://go.fb.me/11ffl7

We evaluated performance across 150+ benchmark datasets across a range of languages — in addition to extensive human evaluations in real-world scenarios. Trained on >16K NVIDIA H100 GPUs, Llama 3.1 405B is the industry leading open source foundation model and delivers state-of-the-art capabilities that rival the best closed source models in general knowledge, steerability, math, tool use and multilingual translation.

We’ve also updated our license to allow developers to use the outputs from Llama models — including the 405B — to improve other models for the first time. We’re excited about how synthetic data generation and model distillation workflows with Llama will help to advance the state of AI.

As Mark Zuckerberg shared this morning, we have a strong belief that open source will ensure that more people around the world have access to the benefits and opportunities of AI and that’s why we continue to take steps on the path for open source AI to become the industry standard.

With these releases we’re setting the stage for unprecedented new opportunities and we can’t wait to see the innovation our newest Llama models will unlock across all levels of the AI community. 💥BOOM 💥 Llama 3.1 is out 💥
405B, 70B, 8B versions.
Main takeaways:
1. 405B performance is on par with the best closed models.
2. Open/free weights and code, with a license that enables fine-tuning, distillation into other models, and deployment anywhere.
3. 128k context length, multi-lingual abilities, good code generation performance, complex reasoning abilities, tool use.
4. Llama Stack API for easy integration.
5. Ecosystem with over 25 partners, including AWS, NVIDIA, Databricks, Groq, Dell, Azure, and Google Cloud.

input: topic: This week on a panel discussing the National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) with Anna Eshoo, Russ Altman, and Dr. | tags: #AI
output:
This week on a panel discussing the National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) with Anna Eshoo, Russ Altman, and Dr. Jennifer King, I was asked about the university brain drain. Is it a real problem? First, let’s acknowledge that talent exchange is a healthy thing. We have a porous structure between private and public sectors that allows people to bring insights in and out of these roles. If that ecosystem is healthy, it’s the secret sauce of why we’re so successful. But what we see now is that the draining is asymmetrical. From what I’ve seen in the Stanford University School of Engineering CS department, incredible researchers are leaving academia to do important work elsewhere. Students watch their friends go to places that have the necessary data and compute resources for #AI. I firmly believe resources like the NAIRR will help rebalance this ecosystem and keep important talent and scientific discovery in the public sector.

input: topic: I loved Kevin Roose’s “Meet my AI friends” article in The New York Times, where he shares his experience exploring AI companions. | tags: #nextgen #HumanFirst
output:
I loved Kevin Roose’s “Meet my AI friends” article in The New York Times, where he shares his experience exploring AI companions. For now, I’m personally sticking with ChatGPT and perplexity :) 

I fundamentally believe that there is a huge opportunity in giving each of us access to personal AI assistants and I really do think these AIs can help us be happier, healthier, more productive and more creative. However, there's a crucial caveat: We need to ensure these AI companions make us more human, not less. 

This is where I have concerns about some current approaches. Take Character.AI, a leading personal AI platform, which reportedly garnered millions of downloads in just a week, according to a recent CB Insights report, with a majority of their users being teenagers! This rapid adoption highlights the demand, but are we simply creating a digital Band-Aid for loneliness?

What if these apps become another distraction, further isolating users?

Think about it: Kids spending eight hours a day interacting with AI friends; adults gravitating to AI romantic partners. I worry that we will look back in five years 10 years and it’s social media all over again - addictive and ultimately unhealthy.

At Blue Tulip Ventures, we are looking for AI startups that are building #nextgen personal AI companions that help you in every aspect of your life, but that are doing so in a responsible and ethical way.

#HumanFirst

input: topic: I’m excited to kick off the first of our short courses focused on agents, starting with Building Agentic RAG with LlamaIndex, taught by Jerry Liu, CEO of LlamaIndex.
output:
I’m excited to kick off the first of our short courses focused on agents, starting with Building Agentic RAG with LlamaIndex, taught by Jerry Liu, CEO of LlamaIndex.

This covers an important shift in RAG (retrieval augmented generation), in which rather than having the developer write explicit routines to retrieve information to feed into the LLM context, we instead build a RAG agent that that has access to tools for retrieving information. This lets the agent decide what information to fetch, and enables it to answer more complex questions using multi-step reasoning.

In detail, you'll learn about:
- Routing: Where your agent will use decision-making to route requests to multiple tools.
- Tool Use: Where you'll create an interface for agents to select what tool (function call) to use as well as generate the right arguments.
- Multi-step reasoning with tool use: Where you'll use an LLM to carry out multiple steps of reasoning, while retaining memory throughout the process.

You’ll also learn how to step through what your agent is doing to debug and improve it iteratively.

It’s an exciting time to build agents. Sign up and get started here! https://lnkd.in/gmvEDidg

topic: 🤖💡 The Unseen Influence of Psychology in AI: A Tribute to Eleanor Rosch

A fascinating insight from Fei-Fei Li's book, "The World I See," brought this to light, highlighting the foundational role of psychologist Eleanor Rosch in shaping AI as we know it. | tags: #ArtificialIntelligence #PsychologyInAI #EleanorRosch #FeiFeiLi #Innovation
output:
🤖💡 The Unseen Influence of Psychology in AI: A Tribute to Eleanor Rosch

A fascinating insight from Fei-Fei Li's book, "The World I See," brought this to light, highlighting the foundational role of psychologist Eleanor Rosch in shaping AI as we know it.

🌐 WordNet, ImageNet, and Beyond: A Psychological Framework
The creation of ImageNet, a cornerstone in AI's journey, owes its structure to the psychological principles embedded in WordNet. This isn't just about labeling; it's about understanding the intricate web of human thoughts and categorizations - a complex, interconnected tree of ideas. This structure mirrors the human mind's way of organizing information, demonstrating how AI, at its best, reflects the depth of human cognition.

🧠 Psychology: The Blueprint for AI's Human-Like Abilities
Eleanor Rosch's work in categorization doesn't just help in image recognition; it extends to natural language processing, decision-making, and emotional intelligence. By integrating such psychological insights, AI not only imitates but also learns like a human, bridging the gap between binary code and human experience.

💭 Final Thoughts
Eleanor Rosch's contributions, as emphasized by Fei-Fei Li, serve as a reminder of the interdisciplinary nature of AI. Psychology isn't just a footnote in AI's history; it's a driving force, shaping the very way we envision and create intelligent systems.

#ArtificialIntelligence #PsychologyInAI #EleanorRosch #FeiFeiLi #Innovation #TechInsights

input: topic: Our paper, "An evidence review of face masks against COVID-19", written by a cross-disciplinary team of 19 international experts, was published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences yesterday.
output:
Our paper, "An evidence review of face masks against COVID-19", written by a cross-disciplinary team of 19 international experts, was published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences yesterday.

The paper, which includes 141 references, argues that we should increase focus on a previously overlooked aspect of mask usage: mask wearing by infectious people ("source control"), rather than only mask wearing by susceptible people ("PPE").

We wrote the first version of this paper back in April, and it became the most viewed paper of all time on any topic on preprints.org. It took nine months to go through peer review and publication - which is a good example of why you shouldn't wait for peer review in a pandemic!

input: topic: Today marks our six-year anniversary! | tags: #ALS
output:
Today marks our six-year anniversary! We recently reflected on our progress and the opportunity ahead at our annual in vivo week, a signature event run by and for insitrocytes to learn, collaborate, and innovate. This year we set a new bar for inspiring scientific advances, progress in our ML models, moving keynote sessions, creative connections, and a little bit of healthy competition.

There were six takeaways that stood out and propel us forward. Read more below. ⬇ This week we mark our 6th anniversary at insitro, and I have been reflecting on a question I get asked a lot as a founder: how do you build culture effectively? That task is particularly challenging when building a diverse team comprising members across multiple disciplines, with different experiences and orthogonal ways of thinking and communicating. 

One way to help create a unified, cohesive community is by bringing everyone together LIVE so we can learn from and inspire each other. As we were emerging from the pandemic in 2022, we started a new tradition we call ‘in vivo week.’ Team members from all over the world come together in person, so we can reaffirm our mission, build connections and learn from outside perspectives.

This year, we were fortunate to host two incredible guests as our keynote speakers. They shed new light on two fundamental takeaways that energized me and the rest of our community of insitrocytes, now close to 300 strong:

First, it's imperative to stay committed to our platform as we build our pipeline. Huge thanks to John Maraganore, the founder of Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and the visionary behind RNA interference technology, who described the 20-year journey of building a company that serves as a model for our “pipeline through platform” ethos.  Our team was truly inspired by his experiences as he shared how he rode hype cycles, faced challenges, and stayed motivated by following the science and the promise of helping patients. 

Second, time is not on patients' side. Enormous gratitude to the brave Brian Wallach and his wife Sandra Abrevaya, who have lived with Brian’s #ALS diagnosis for the last six years. To help all ALS patients, they founded I AM ALS, which has helped raise more than $1 billion for new research. They also founded Synapticure to provide care for people living with neurodegenerative conditions and help meet the urgent need for more patient data to drive discovery. Hearing their story, there was not a dry eye in the house.

In vivo week brought us closer than ever and reinforced my conviction and sense of purpose when I first founded insitro. Patients are waiting and we need to continue our journey with courage and urgency, in order to help them.

input: topic: It seems to me that before "urgently figuring out how to control AI systems much smarter than us" we need to have the beginning of a hint of a design for a system smarter than a house cat.
output:
It seems to me that before "urgently figuring out how to control AI systems much smarter than us" we need to have the beginning of a hint of a design for a system smarter than a house cat.

Such a misplaced sense of urgency reveals an extremely distorted view of reality.
No wonder the more based members of the organization seeked to marginalize the superalignment group.

It's as if someone had said in 1925 "we urgently need to figure out how to control aircrafts that can transport hundreds of passengers at near the speed of sound over the oceans."
It would have been difficult to make long-haul passenger jets safe before the turbojet was invented and before any aircraft had crossed the atlantic non-stop.
Yet, we can now fly halfway around the world on twin-engine jets in complete safety. 
It didn't require some sort of magical recipe for safety.
It took decades of careful engineering and iterative refinements.

The process will be similar for intelligent systems.
It will take years for them to get as smart as cats, and more years to get as smart as humans, let alone smarter (don't confuse the superhuman knowledge accumulation and retrieval abilities of current LLMs with actual intelligence).
It will take years for them to be deployed and fine-tuned for efficiency and safety as they are made smarter and smarter.

https://lnkd.in/eaJ5uuMk Approaching the AI control system question from the perspective of fear is clickbait. Talking about "smarter than us" without defining "smarter" is clickbait. Machines have better memory than us and can operate at mind-boggling scale. Already. Today.

And technology is getting more complex rapidly.

So how about we approach the question of control from the perspective of how to make complex tech useful and who should be qualified to steer it?

We need systems designed to help steer complex technology (AI and human-made) in a way that is useful, safe, and effective... and doesn't require the people leading and executing projects to have graduate degrees in AI or AI-adjacent fields. 

So when I say control systems, here's what I mean: a way to effectively steer complex things in a world that's getting increasingly complex. The fact that they'd help you with AGI (one day) is a byproduct. There are plenty of other things that need steering and we seem to have collectively forgotten there's no steering wheel.

Shouldn't we be ashamed of building systems that are far reaching in nature (already) without also building the tools that allow them to be shaped and steered by the people most qualified to lead and the domain experts most qualified to understand the nuances of the context we're launching them into? Applied AI leadership shouldn't require a graduate degree in an AI-adjacent field. Surely that's a tipoff that our control tools aren't good enough yet. Surely we should be embarrassed that we haven't annihilated the boundary between the technical and the non-technical yet. We clearly need better tools to form a solid control system. A system that guarantees tech offers what those in charge actually wanted, not what they said they wanted, while also representing the laws and needs of society.

And I don't see a control system that allows a standard senior executive (non-technical, but with domain expertise) to participate well in AI design, development, and testing. To get their perspective on their organization's needs, preferences, priorities, and risk tolerances represented and executed on effectively. Frankly, I don't see many solutions for even testing complex systems, so no wonder all these attempts at "enterprise scale GenAI without a human in the loop" keep dying not with a bang but with a whimper. How are we going to trust it with anything important if we can't test it?

I believe the path to leadership in tomorrow's world shouldn't require a STEM background. It should require a leadership background and the right tools for those leaders to build/buy the tech they need and control it so it works as *they* need it to. I call that a control system. And we don't have one yet.

input: topic: Here’s a little AI history lesson for you to kick off the week...
output:
Here’s a little AI history lesson for you to kick off the week...

68 years ago, four researchers submitted a grant seeking funding for AI research. (Fun fact, this is the first known use of the term ‘artificial intelligence’.). Here’s what they wrote:

“An attempt will be made to find how to make machines use language, form abstractions and concepts, solve kinds of problems now reserved for humans, and improve themselves. We think that a significant advance can be made in one or more of these problems if a carefully selected group of scientists work on it together for a summer." 

- Proposal for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence (McCarthy et al, 1955)

A summer! In 1956!

It’s ok if you sometimes find yourself overwhelmed by the pace of progress, or the grand proclamations of AGI. Just remind yourself this industry has leaned overoptimistic on timelines from its very start.

input: topic: Today’s AI revolution isn’t an AI revolution at all.
output:
Today’s AI revolution isn’t an AI revolution at all. It’s a UX revolution.

If you used Netflix or Google products 10 years ago, you interacted with AI. But you weren’t supposed to know it. 

The predominant design philosophy of the time centered on hiding the AI but delivering magical moments like tailored movie recommendations and autocompleted email copy. GenAI changed all that.

Now, the AI systems are put directly into the hands of the end users. Everyone can be the builder; anyone can create a custom graphic, website, or entire app just about instantly. 

ChatGPT did a great job with creating a chat interface, but we haven’t even cracked the surface on the kinds of user interfaces that are possible. I can’t wait to see how this evolves. 

If you’re building interesting GenAI UX, tell me about it in the comments! 

I also expand on these topics in Samuel Durand's documentary about the future of work. You can check out a clip below, or watch the full documentary here: https://lnkd.in/eZfvEmT9

input: topic: Bravo to IBM on everything launched at Think 2024 this week. | tags: #Think2024 #IBMpartner
output:
Bravo to IBM on everything launched at Think 2024 this week. They’re getting a lot of things right; let's talk about it 👇

There's so much talk these days about when AI will become smarter than us. But I'm far more interested in what tools we're building that give us control of complex technology. I want control systems that anyone can use - everyone from a machine learning engineer to a business leader or non technical sales person - to steer this technology and maximize their data to make better decisions.

IBM is a proven leader in building these kinds of tools so it was a delight to stop by the Think Conference in Boston this week. It's clear they're laser focused on making it easier for large orgs to integrate GenAI securely and are building ** control systems ** to make it happen. As they told me, their purview is to make AI "more open, cost effective, and flexible for businesses." That's what we want!

Here are some of the best things I saw at THINK:

1. IBM Concert: 
- Think of Concert as the nerve center for enterprise automation. Powered by GenAI, Concert looks into all of your applications, predicts potential problems or security issues, and suggests fixes or enhancements.
- What I like about Concert is that it visualizes everything in a way that's easy to understand for technical and non technical folks alike. It shows you a clear view of what models you have and how they're connected. This is a really important step in how we enable more members of enterprise teams to manage really complex technology.
- If you're in security risk management, compliance management, or certificate management take a look at Concert; they're starting with those applications first. You can check out more about Concert here: https://ibm.biz/BdmesT

2. Open source models
- IBM made a big effort to highlight their open-source efforts.
- They released a family of IBM granite models into open source, including their best performing Code LLMs.
- It's great proof that the largest model isn't always best! Granite is a smaller model that scrubs out undesirable content to be hyper performant.
- They're also partnering with Red Hat to launch InstructLab, a model alignment technique for bringing open source community contributions directly into LLMs.

3. AI Governance
- watsonx is IBM's AI and data platform for unifying enterprise data. It helps large organizations scale and govern AI models securely.
- Governance and regulations are becoming far more complicated and changing all the time. watsonx lets you build tech that's compliant, and stays up to date with any regulatory changes.
- I'm really excited to see IBM leading the way on governance so we avoid getting bogged down in compliance and can just keep building.

I did some vlogging on the show floor, so look out for a deeper dive into IBM's new products soon. Until then, you can read more about all of IBM's newest launches here: https://ibm.biz/Bdmesx 

#Think2024 #IBMpartner

input: topic: LLMs can take gigabytes of memory to store, which limits what can be run on consumer hardware.
output:
LLMs can take gigabytes of memory to store, which limits what can be run on consumer hardware. But quantization can dramatically compress models, making a wider selection of models available to developers. You can often reduce model size by 4x or more while maintaining reasonable performance. In our new short course Quantization Fundamentals taught by Hugging Face's Younes Belkada and Marc Sun, you'll: 
- Learn how to quantize nearly any open source model
- Use int8 and bfloat16 (Brain float 16) data types to load and run LLMs using PyTorch and the Hugging Face Transformers library
- Dive into the technical details of linear quantization to map 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers

As models get bigger and bigger, quantization becomes more important for making models practical and accessible. Please check out the course here:  https://lnkd.in/g66yNW8W

input: topic: As we reach the milestone of the 256th issue of The Batch, I’m reflecting on how AI has changed over the years and how society continues to change with it.
output:
As we reach the milestone of the 256th issue of The Batch, I’m reflecting on how AI has changed over the years and how society continues to change with it. As AI becomes more widely available, it’s clear that many people — developers and non-developers — will benefit from high-quality training to keep up with the changes and gain useful AI skills.

In my years of working in education, I’ve felt that the world has enough low-quality courses, newsletters, social media posts, and other forms of content.  It’s possible to build a business churning out mediocre content in sufficient volume to attract a meaningful amount of attention, but I have no interest in doing that.

At DeepLearning.AI, our core philosophy is to put learners first. Our team obsesses about how to create quality training or other programs that benefit people who want to learn about AI. We have intense debates about what tools to teach, which examples to include, even which partners to work with, based on what we think is best for learners.

For example, I recall vividly how, when working on the Machine Learning Specialization, our team spent ages debating whether to use row or column matrices. Both sides showed up with deep analysis of the pros and cons, made Powerpoint presentations to argue their case, and we spent hours debating over what was better for learners in terms of both ease of picking up the concepts as well as subsequently being able to use these skills with third-party machine learning libraries.

We don’t release a course unless we think it’s a good use of a learner’s time and we’d be proud to recommend it to our own friends and family members. Quality, of course, can mean a lot of things. I expect what we do to be technically accurate, useful, up to date, clear, and time-efficient for learners. And, if possible, fun!

We don’t always get it right, but we scrutinize learner feedback (one of my most important weekly routines is to study a dashboard that summarizes learner ratings of our courses) and work to make sure our courses serve learners well. And yes, we have a large-language model powered application that reads learner reviews to flag important issues quickly.

Earlier this year, we realized that some of the paid content we had launched was below our quality standard, and that I wouldn’t in good conscience recommend it to my friends or family members. Despite this content being profitable, we did what we felt was the right thing for learners. So we decided to retire that content and forgo the revenues, but we feel much better now for having done the right thing for learners.

input: topic: Just wrapped up the a360 summit in LA. | tags: #emotionAI #AI #women
output:
Just wrapped up the a360 summit in LA. What an INSPIRING few days. Peter H. Diamandis, I said this before but will say it again: I feel so lucky to be orbiting in your universe! 

I had this aha moment at this summit: We need wholesome AI. What if we didn’t just build AI that is smarter … but instead built wholesome AI. 

When something is wholesome, it is good for our health and well-being. It is good for the environment and our planet. It is trustworthy and morally sound. And it is beautiful in its diversity and inclusivity. Wholesome AI is good for people - all people.  

I spent the last 20 years pioneering #emotionAI and humanizing technology before it dehumanizes. First in Academia at University of Cambridge and Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and then later at Affectiva and Smart Eye. 

Marrying AI’s IQ with EQ is a key ingredient of making #AI wholesome. I loved hearing the theme of Empathy and EQ in AI come up over and over again at the summit. YAY to that! 

But I also has this realization: AI today is not wholesome (yet). In particular, it is lacking diversity in the founders and builders of AI, and as a result, it is lacking in diversity of ideas. I am on a mission to change that. And I am SO grateful that Peter H. Diamandis has publicly committed to the Abundance360 community to continue to give a platform to #women in AI. This year, a360 launched the inaugural women’s fellow program, which I was part of alongside some amazing women. 

But the summit highlighted that we still have MAJOR work to do. The first 1.5 days of the summit were focused on AI, but only 2 out of 22 speakers on stage were women. The fact is: there simply aren’t enough women builders, founders and CEOs in AI today. 

Just look at the founders and CEOs of the top AI companies or the most funded AI companies. Being at the summit strengthened my resolve to be a catalyst for change. We absolutely need more women and underrepresented founders building the next generation of AI companies. 

I will leave you with this quote from actor and poet Sekou Andrews “What if AI knows that humanity is the one thing it can enhance …. but can never inherit”.

input: topic: In the latest episode of the AI Grand Rounds podcast, Dr. | tags: #ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #AIinMedicine
output:
In the latest episode of the AI Grand Rounds podcast, Dr. Daphne Koller charts her professional trajectory, tracing her early fascination with computers to her influential role in #ArtificialIntelligence and health care. 

Initially intrigued by the capacity of computers for decision-making based on theoretical principles, Koller witnessed her niche area — once considered peripheral to AI — grow to dominate the field. Her curiosity led her from abstract theory to practical #MachineLearning applications and eventually to the complex world of biomedicine.

Throughout the podcast, Koller shares her shift from pure computer science to the integration of machine learning in biological and medical research. She explains the unique challenges of applying AI to biology, distinguishing it from more deterministic fields, and how these complexities feed into her work at insitro, where she is leveraging AI throughout the drug discovery and development process, from disease understanding to therapeutic application and monitoring. She advocates for the democratizing potential of AI, underscoring its capacity to enable broader participation in scientific inquiry and problem-solving. 
 
Listen to the full episode hosted by NEJM AI Deputy Editors Arjun Manrai, PhD, and Andrew Beam, PhD: https://nejm.ai/ep17
 
#AIinMedicine Thank you, Arjun Manrai and Andrew Beam for a great conversation on what is happening - and what could happen in the future - when AI meets biology and healthcare!

input: topic: On Father’s Day last weekend, I sat with my daughter to help her practice solving arithmetic problems.
output:
On Father’s Day last weekend, I sat with my daughter to help her practice solving arithmetic problems. To give her practice problems, I used OpenDevin, an open-source agentic coding framework, to write a Python script that generated questions that she enjoyed answering at her own pace. OpenDevin wrote the code much faster than I could have and genuinely improved my and my daughter’s day. 

Six months ago, coding agents were a novelty. They still frequently fail to deliver, but I find that they’re now working well enough that they might be genuinely useful to more and more people! 

Given a coding problem that’s specified in a prompt, the workflow for a coding agent typically goes something like this: Use a large language model (LLM) to analyze the problem and potentially break it into steps to write code for, generate the code, test it, and iteratively use any errors discovered to ask the coding agent to refine its answer. But within this broad framework, a huge design space and numerous innovations are available to experiment with. I’d like to highlight a few papers that I find notable:
- “AgentCoder: Multiagent-Code Generation with Iterative Testing and Optimisation,” Huang et al. (2024). 
- “LDB: A Large Language Model Debugger via Verifying Runtime Execution Step by Step,” Zhong et al., (2024). 
- “SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering,” Yang et al. (2024).

How can we test the code without requiring the user to write test cases? In a multi-agent system, each “agent” is an LLM prompted to play a particular role. An interesting result from AgentCoder shows that having separate agents for writing code and generating tests results in better performance than letting a single agent do both tasks. This is presumably because, if the agent writing the code is also responsible for writing the tests, the tests might be influenced by the code and fail to consider corner cases that the code does not cover. 
When people think of testing code, many initially think of output testing, in which we see if the code generates the correct outputs to a specific set of test inputs. If the code fails a test, an LLM can be prompted to reflect on why the code failed and then to try to fix it. In addition to testing the output, the LDB method is helpful. LDB steps through the code and presents to the LLM values of the variables during intermediate steps of execution, to see if the LLM can spot exactly where the error is. This mimics how a human developer might step through the code to see where one of the computational steps went wrong, and so pinpoint and fix the problem. 

A lot of agentic workflows mimic human workflows. Similar to other work in machine learning, if humans can do a task, then trying to mimic humans makes development much easier compared to inventing a new process. [...]

input: topic: We reached a DEAL on the #AIAct! | tags: #AIAct #trilogue #ArtificialIntelligence #AI #AIAct
output:
We reached a DEAL on the #AIAct! ✅

Almost two years of difficult negotiations including over 33 hours in the latest #trilogue. Now, we have a political agreement. 

Our talks have led us to a common European vision: One in which #ArtificialIntelligence is more democratic, safe and competitive.

We succeeded in putting in place guardrails to ensure AI systems are safe and that our democratic values are protected. At the same time, I have pushed hard for - and I believe we have achieved - a reasonable balance between safety and openness to innovation. 

We know that AI is the next big thing when it comes to economic growth and productivity. Europe needs to be on top of it. AI can and will be an engine for European competitiveness. This is why we have tried to provide the conditions for European companies to innovate:  
👉Ambitious approach to regulatory sandboxes 
👉Flexible and agile approach to general purpose AI models 
👉Research and open source exemptions
👉OECD definition of AI system 
👉Narrow scope tailored to only true high-risk use cases 
👉Alignment with existing sectoral legislation to reduce regulatory burden
👉Improved measures for SMEs including start-ups
👉More realistic and feasible requirements for companies

We must not forget that the #AI Act was put on the table to establish guardrails and regulate risky use cases. We have included the above pro-innovation measures in the text but the EU can still benefit from a more ambitious strategy to fostering AI excellence in Europe.  

Crucial steps lay ahead - including fine-tuning of the text and the adoption of a final deal by both the European Parliament and the Member States. The time after that - once we get there - will be even more crucial to successful implementation through guidelines, standards, etc. 

For the time being, however, this political agreement is a BIG step forward. We have set a common vision for democratic development of AI, one that is in line with our international partners. 

Thank you to all my colleagues, to the teams involved, to all the experts and stakeholders, for the engagement. We continue the work ahead.

#AIAct #trilogue #artificialintelligence #legislation #global #Europe #ai #tech #digital #governance #aigovernance #tech #techcommunity #innovation The EU #AIAct is here!

input: topic: AI research is currently dominated by a few extremely well-funded companies.
output:
AI research is currently dominated by a few extremely well-funded companies. 
Australian AI researcher Jeremy Howard wants to change that.

In December he co-founded Answer.AI alongside The Long Term Stock Exchange creator Eric Ries with a goal to creating an R&D lab that makes useful AI products tailor made for professionals across industries like law, media, medicine and development. 

That's different to the likes of OpenAI and Anthropic, Howard told me, where products like ChatGPT come as a by-product of their overarching mission to develop artificial general intelligence (AGI). 

Overnight Answer.AI closed an US$8 million angel investment funding round that includes Google Gemini heads Jeff Dean and Oriol Vinyals, as well as the founders of AI unicorns Hugging Face, Cohere and Together AI. That's on top of the US$10 million Answer.AI raised in December from Decibel Partners. 

“Our role models here are the early electricity hackers, people successfully applying electricity at a point when its utility was pretty much as unknown as AI is today,” Howard said. “Everyone’s gone chatbot crazy, but we’re interested in building things other than chatbots.”

input: topic: Ten years ago, I started a tradition with my kids as we were relocating from Cairo to Boston.
output:
Ten years ago, I started a tradition with my kids as we were relocating from Cairo to Boston. Jana was 10 and Adam was 4, and I was desperate to make our move fun and meaningful. And so on our long flight over, we pulled out my laptop and worked on our goals for the year. 

Every year since then, we spend the week leading up to New Year’s sequestered (ideally somewhere warm!) reflecting on the previous year and getting intentional about the next one. And dare I say, it is one of my personal favorite activities of the year. 

Not only is it an opportunity to take stock of all our blessings and to be grateful, but we also get intentional about how and where we want to spend our energy in the coming year. Contrary to popular belief (aka I am not this Monster Mom locking up her kids to goal set LoL), this exercise is SUPER fun. We get quite creative, pushing each other to think big and outside the box. We tease each other but we also get quite vulnerable with each other, sharing our lowlights and hardest lessons we learnt throughout the year. 

This year, Adam took charge as our facilitator and he CRUSHED it. First, to avoid getting stuck in “incremental” goals, we moved our review of this year’s goals to the end, and added a new Moonshots & Crazy Ideas section. Second, we noticed that you can goal set all you want, but unless you implement systems in place that allow you turn your goal into something actionable, it’s just a goal (Credit goes to my coach Trevor Timbeck for this one). 

Finally, we noticed that annual goals are often focused on the self. But it’s way more powerful to think about “what are you doing for the world” goals. How are you creating positive impact in your family, school, community and beyond.

So here’s our 5 step exercise: 

- Visualization: It is the 31st of December 2024. What does your world look like? What have you built? Accomplished? Who are you spending time with? How do you feel? 

- Intentions and Goals: Write down your goals for the year. Be specific. Some categories we like to be intentional about: Health & wellness, spirituality & inner peace, family, love & relationships, professional and academic goals, personal development, financial goals, community & impact goals.

- Moonshot and crazy ideas. Think big. Like what would be outrageous and seemingly impossible to go do. Great!  Now, write those down :) 

- Reflect on the previous year. What were your highlights? What were the biggest achievements? What did you accomplish that weren’t even on your horizon a year ago (in my case for example, it is the Eisenhower Fellowships) What was the hardest lessons and what did you learn from it? What one thing you did that may impact? How did you fare against your goals from last year? What are you most grateful for? 

- Theme of the year. Capture all of this into one word that encapsulates your energy for the year.

Here’s to 2024, Happy New Year! Wishing you all an amazing year ahead.

Jana Amin

output:
Per Yann LeCun & Demis Hassabis - the current evolution of LLMs is superficial and limited, with the models learning only when human engineers intervene to train it on that information. 

Agree vehemently - human reasoning is not algorithmic (read Dan Ariely etc.) and sentiments tend to be intangible not probability based (mental health, wellbeing, dating apps or divorce stats anyone?). "... Systems hallucinate, they don't really understand the real world. They require enormous amounts of data to reach a level of [perceived] intelligence. And they can't really reason. #Ethics, #governance, costs etc aside, I embrace the current wave of #innovation, and find the current democratization of 'AI' amazing. However, I'd advocate for the next evolution of #AGI enhancing rather than replicating human behaviour because there is nothing general about human intelligence.

input: topic: Because LLMs are unreliable, I struggle to see how (or why) we will use them for clinical tasks that matter.
output:
Because LLMs are unreliable, I struggle to see how (or why) we will use them for clinical tasks that matter. This fantastic interview helped me see things more clearly. 

Francois Chollet explains that LLMs are memorization and pattern matching machines. While this is quite useful, it is not the same as intelligence, which he defines as “the ability to adapt to an ever-changing world.”

(Piaget similarly defined intelligence as “what you use when you don’t know what to do”).

Because memorization and pattern matching are limited, Francois argues for hybrid systems that merge LLMs/deep learning with other approaches (e.g., discrete program search) to, in essence, mirror what Kahneman described as System 1- and System 2-type thinking.

I'll let the real computer scientists elaborate or tell me why this is wrong. 

To me, the point is that if we ever broadly use LLMs for clinical tasks where errors matter, they will likely be combined with other types of AI. (Other leading computer scientists like Stephen Wolfram also argue for this). Spencer Dorn you are actually right. As Yann LeCun says, LLMs are "just" enormous autocomplete engines. When they are used to draft something, with systematic clinician review, that's OK. But we shouldn't use them for medical decisions when even 99.9% accuracy is not enough.

I agree with François Chollet that the solution is to combine LLMs (we should be careful with anthropomorphism but I like to think of them as the creative, subconscious part of the brain) with more rigid models (that would be the prefrontal cortex) that reason and "filter" the output of the LLM. Yann LeCun also suggests this approach.

At Nabla we are actually using such an approach to prevent hallucinations. It's inspired from Ian Goodfellow’s Generative Adversarial Networks, where two very different models compete against each other.

input: topic: ​​Planning is a key agentic AI design pattern in which we use a large language model (LLM) to autonomously decide on what sequence of steps to execute to accomplish a larger task.
output:
​​Planning is a key agentic AI design pattern in which we use a large language model (LLM) to autonomously decide on what sequence of steps to execute to accomplish a larger task. For example, if we ask an agent to do online research on a given topic, we might use an LLM to break down the objective into smaller subtasks, such as researching specific subtopics, synthesizing findings, and compiling a report.

Many people had a “ChatGPT moment” shortly after ChatGPT was released, when they were surprised that it significantly exceeded their expectation of what AI can do. If you have not yet had a similar “AI Agentic moment,” I hope you will soon! I had one several months ago, when I presented a live demo of a research agent I had implemented that had access to various online search tools.

I had tested this agent multiple times privately, during which it consistently used a web search tool to gather information and wrote up a summary. During the live demo, the web search API unexpectedly returned with a rate limiting error. I thought my demo was about to fail publicly. To my surprise, the agent pivoted deftly to a Wikipedia search tool — which I had forgotten I’d given it — and completed the task using Wikipedia instead of web search.

This was an AI Agentic moment of surprise for me. It’s a beautiful thing when you see an agent autonomously do things in ways that you had not anticipated, and succeed as a result!

Many tasks can’t be done in a single step. For example, to simplify an example from the HuggingGPT paper (cited below), if you want an agent to examine a boy's picture and draw a picture of a girl in the same pose, the task might be decomposed into two steps: (i) detect the boy's pose and (ii) render a picture of a girl in the detected pose. An LLM might be fine-tuned or prompted (with few-shot prompting) to specify a plan by outputting a string like "{tool: pose-detection, input: image.jpg, output: temp1 } {tool: pose-to-image, input: temp1, output: final.jpg}".

This structured output triggers software to invoke a pose detection tool followed by a pose-to-image tool to complete the task. (This example is for illustrative purposes only; HuggingGPT uses a different format.)

Admittedly, many agentic workflows do not need planning. For example, you might have an agent reflect on, and improve, its output a fixed number of times,  resulting in a set of fixed, deterministic steps. But for complex tasks in which you can't specify a task decomposition ahead of time, Planning allows the agent to decide dynamically what steps to take.

To learn more, I recommend:
- Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models, Wei et al. (2022)
- HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face, Shen et al. (2023)
- Understanding the planning of LLM agents: A survey, by Huang et al. (2024)

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/gM2ZWNsW ]

input: topic: If you work in #AI #Policy or live in #California you may or may not know about the attempt to ban #OpenSource AI models in #SB1047. | tags: #AI #Policy #California #OpenSource #SB1047
output:
If you work in #AI #Policy or live in #California you may or may not know about the attempt to ban #OpenSource AI models in #SB1047.

This is another regulatory capture bill, where large vendors attempt to kill competitors by claiming safety concerns (Absurd of course: most #AIEthics work is done using open source models because researchers need the ability to inspect and modify the model). 

The California SB1047 bill will kill open source models in California. But legislators don't realize this because they've been told the carve outs in the bill are sufficient. Apparently the research community hasn't communicated how easy it is to change the behavior of a #LLM with small amounts of training. 

Jeremy Howard wrote an excellent piece on this here: https://lnkd.in/gisHcJ_A. The important parts are these:

* California’s SB 1047 bill requires, under penalty of perjury, the developers assure their “model has does not have a hazardous capability”, even if up to 25% of the weights are changed
* Senator Scott Wiener, the primary backer of the bill, says that they crafted the bill “in a way that ensures that open source developers are able to comply”.

and "It is not possible to provide assurance that a released model can’t be used to cause harm, because a released model can be changed."

The best way to regulate AI is like this:

"We can still regulate AI, and allow open source. To do so, we can regulate use of AI, rather than development of AI. If someone uses AI for election interference, or discriminatory resume screening, or deep fakes, for instance, that usage can be made illegal."

Please share with policy makers!

output:
[Cérémonie] 🎓 Ce soir, mercredi 26 juin 2024, ESIEE PARIS décerne son premier titre de Professeur Honoris Causa à Yann LeCun, diplômé de l’école, prix Turing, vice-président et « chief AI scientist » chez Meta. Yann Le Cun est également professeur à l’université de New-York.

En présence de Jean Mairesse, Directeur Général de ESIEE Paris, et Daniel NABET, Président du Conseil d'école ESIEE Paris, Yann Le Cun a été honoré pour avoir façonné l'avenir de l'intelligence artificielle avec ses travaux pionniers sur le deep learning. 

Ses innovations, dont les premières versions de l'algorithme de rétropropagation du gradient développées à ESIEE Paris, ont révolutionné la vision par ordinateur, le traitement du langage naturel et bien plus encore. Cette distinction marque un moment historique alors que ESIEE Paris célèbre ses 120 ans. 🌐✨

Entre Yann Le Cun et ESIEE Paris, les collaborations se poursuivent : co-encadrement de thèses, co-écriture d’articles… Il est également Président du Conseil scientifique de ESIEE Paris, alignant la recherche de l’école avec celle de l’Université Gustave Eiffel. 

🎥 Découvrez la web-série produite par ESIEE Paris en 2019 qui retrace l’histoire commune entre Yann Le Cun et ESIEE Paris : https://lnkd.in/gqE3K2Jz
👉 Voir le communiqué de presse : https://lnkd.in/e5vwBaYw

#distinction #IA #DeepLearning #HonorisCausa #enseignement #Turing #IntelligenceArtificielle #AI Thank you to my alma mater ESIEE PARIS for awarding me the inaugural title of Professor Honoris Causa.

I graduated from ESIEE in 1983, with a major in integrated circuit design.
The plaque is actually a silicon wafer with the inscription etched in a layer of gold, produced in ESIEE's clean room!

input: topic: Recently, I met some incredible young women at Harvard Undergraduate Women in Entrepreneurship’s inaugural Female Founder's Circle. | tags: #Entrepreneurship #women #tech #AI
output:
Recently, I met some incredible young women at Harvard Undergraduate Women in Entrepreneurship’s inaugural Female Founder's Circle. Talk about a powerhouse network! The passion and drive were contagious. 

#Entrepreneurship can be a wild ride, and fear of risk is a hurdle many #women face. But here's the truth I shared with these future leaders:

* You are your biggest obstacle. Believe in yourself!

* Persistence is key. If you have conviction, don’t take no for an answer. 

* Prioritize self-care. Leaders underestimate how contagious their energy is on their teams. It took me a lot of years to prioritize self-care and in doing so, positively affect those around me.

* Fortune favors the bold - I am a big believer in serendipity, but it is not accidental. Be in situations where you can create opportunities for yourself. 

* Surround yourself with the right communities and mentorship. 

The future for female founders is already here. Women are breaking down barriers. Excited for the next generation of women in #tech and #AI to lead.

A big thank you to the organizers and fellow speakers Daniela Andrade, Mary Catherine Hanafee LaPlante, Elizabeth Brahin, Katie McCormick Lelyveld, Cindy Gallop, Marie Meslin, Lisa Frusztajer, Shannyn A. S., Jennifer Jordan, Cheri Ackerman, Katie McCormick Lelyveld, Kelley Arena and Kris Strouthopoulos.

output:
"Starting in high school or college, I have lived my life according to what I call Verbally Stated Goals (VSG). At each major juncture, I focus on the most core emotion at that time; I crystallize it into words forming a mission statement; and then I tell everyone I meet that this is what I am doing. Telling people my Verbally Stated Goal does two things: first, by declaring it in front of many people, this commits me to achieving the goal; second, when people know what I’m trying to do, they find ways to help me."
Adam Cheyer

input: topic: Imagine a world where a single person can achieve what used to take a team of ten software engineers. | tags: #AI #claimyourclone
output:
Imagine a world where a single person can achieve what used to take a team of ten software engineers. That's the future AI is ushering in, with an exposition in creativity, efficiency and productivity on a per person basis… one of the highlights from the Abundance360 Summit this year Peter H. Diamandis. 

There’s never been a better time to start a company as AI evolves from powerful language models to "large action models" that can translate ideas into reality. 

But it doesn’t stop there. #AI will revolutionize science and biology, including drug discovery and therapeutics. Today, it takes on average 17 years for a scientific discovery to make its way to physicians. Well, AI can hugely reduce this timeline as it can ingest scientific discoveries in a matter of minutes. I am also excited for the application of AI in the discovery of new material, chemistry and physics. 

At the summit, I loved hosting a session with Natalie Monbiot on the emerging virtual human economy. We showed examples of people putting their AI self to work to earn passive income. For instance, your AI self could teach online lectures, and do so in a language you don’t even speak in real life! 

Celebrities are at the forefront of this, licensing their voice to video games. Of course, while these cloning technologies can be empowering, they also present risks: how do you retain control over your virtual identity? How can we detect misuse, and how can we protect against it? In short: How do we make AI cloning work for us and not against us? #claimyourclone 

I’m also extremely proud of my son Adam, who rocked facilitating the teen program this year, bringing youth voices into the mix. I even got to hang out at their table! We explored ways these young people are using AI in their daily lives, and how they plan to use AI to achieve their goals. 

We also had the opportunity to visit Machani Robots. As adults, we often get trapped in the uncanny valley of these robots. But kids? They engage so comfortably with these social robots. We even got Ria to sing “Shake it off” by Taylor Swift. 

But the biggest takeaway I brought home from Abundance 360: be BOLD. Be relentless in pursuit of your dreams. Jason Ballard, the founder of ICON, left me speechless with his vision of tackling poverty with 3D-printed houses – beautiful, affordable, and built in record time. Then there was Dean Kamen, the inventor behind the Segway and the first insulin pump for diabetics. He brought the entire room to tears with his mission to unite kids from across the globe in an annual robotics competition. 

These are the stories that stay with you. Abundance 360 wasn't just about the future, it was about the incredible humans who are shaping it. And that is a future worth getting excited about.

input: topic: Last week, I described four design patterns for AI agentic workflows that I believe will drive significant progress: Reflection, Tool use, Planning and Multi-agent collaboration.
output:
Last week, I described four design patterns for AI agentic workflows that I believe will drive significant progress: Reflection, Tool use, Planning and Multi-agent collaboration. Instead of having an LLM generate its final output directly, an agentic workflow prompts the LLM multiple times, giving it opportunities to build step by step to higher-quality output. Here, I'd like to discuss Reflection. It's relatively quick to implement, and I've seen it lead to surprising performance gains. 

You may have had the experience of prompting ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini, receiving unsatisfactory output, delivering critical feedback to help the LLM improve its response, and then getting a better response. What if you automate the step of delivering critical feedback, so the model automatically criticizes its own output and improves its response? This is the crux of Reflection. 

Take the task of asking an LLM to write code. We can prompt it to generate the desired code directly to carry out some task X. Then, we can prompt it to reflect on its own output, perhaps as follows:

Here’s code intended for task X:
[previously generated code]
Check the code carefully for correctness, style, and efficiency, and give constructive criticism for how to improve it.

Sometimes this causes the LLM to spot problems and come up with constructive suggestions. Next, we can prompt the LLM with context including (i) the previously generated code and (ii) the constructive feedback, and ask it to use the feedback to rewrite the code. This can lead to a better response. Repeating the criticism/rewrite process might yield further improvements. This self-reflection process allows the LLM to spot gaps and improve its output on a variety of tasks including producing code, writing text, and answering questions.

And we can go beyond self-reflection by giving the LLM tools that help evaluate its output; for example, running its code through a few unit tests to check whether it generates correct results on test cases or searching the web to double-check text output. Then it can reflect on any errors it found and come up with ideas for improvement.

Further, we can implement Reflection using a multi-agent framework. I've found it convenient to create two agents, one prompted to generate good outputs and the other prompted to give constructive criticism of the first agent's output. The resulting discussion between the two agents leads to improved responses.

Reflection is a relatively basic type of agentic workflow, but I've been delighted by how much it improved my applications’ results. If you’re interested in learning more about reflection, I recommend:
- Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback, by Madaan et al. (2023)
- Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning, by Shinn et al. (2023)
- CRITIC: Large Language Models Can Self-Correct with Tool-Interactive Critiquing, by Gou et al. (2024)

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/g4bTuWtU ]

input: topic: One of my biggest learnings from my career at Microsoft was that we should have open-sourced more of the work that my team did there.
output:
One of my biggest learnings from my career at Microsoft was that we should have open-sourced more of the work that my team did there. We had an internal C++ library called VisionTools that was a building block for lots of great products: Photosynth, Windows Photo Gallery, and Kinect to name a few. The library currently sits barely used now in some internal repository. I'm convinced that it could have instead powered many more projects out in the open world. But that was a different time and luckily the industry now has many more open-source proponents.

So, I'm thrilled that Ocean, the core image-processing and CV C++ library that we use at Meta, is now open-source. This library was specifically engineered to meet the requirements of building apps on mobile devices. It has a small binary footprint, is optimized for mobile processors, and has some nice examples to get started quickly with things like processing a live camera feed. Ocean is used across all of Meta's apps (Facebook, IG, Messenger, WhatsApp) as well as the Quest VR headsets. I'm excited to see what people build with it! Yes, nothing more frustrating than seeing an awesome piece of software you contributed to sitting on a digital shelf, gathering digital dust, ignored, because your employers doesn't believe in open source.
I suffered from this at AT&T.
One big reason I'm at Meta is that open sourcing infrastructure software and research code is in the DNA of the company.

input: topic: Recently, tech social media blew up with a discussion of whether we need AI control systems (or whatever you want to call them*).
output:
Recently, tech social media blew up with a discussion of whether we need AI control systems (or whatever you want to call them*). Again.

The sensationalist takes could be approximately summarized as:

“We’d better do something urgently so AGI doesn’t outsmart us!”
versus
“AI is still pretty dumb, chill out.”

I’d like to offer a perpendicular perspective in the linked blog post I wrote for you. (In which we both chill out and build something.) In short:

"Nevermind AGI, GenAI, or anything AI. We're not smart enough to design, build, and steer complex technology responsibly and technology is getting more complex quickly. Let's build tools that make us more capable."

Or, more bluntly: 

"Have you noticed that the people in charge are never the human-in-the-loop? There's a loop they need to be in... and they're not there."

*For the semantics fan club, I put a glossary of 57 AI-control-related terms for you at the bottom of the blog post. If you're tempted to argue about word meanings, please enjoy those first. 57 of them should surely provide all the nuance you can guzzle in one go. And color-coded too! <3
