input: topic: One reason for machine learning’s success is that our field welcomes a wide range of work.
output:
One reason for machine learning’s success is that our field welcomes a wide range of work. I can’t think of even one example where someone developed what they called a machine learning algorithm and senior members of our community criticized it saying, “that’s not machine learning!” Indeed, linear regression using a least-squares cost function was used by mathematicians Legendre and Gauss in the early 1800s — long before the invention of computers — yet machine learning has embraced these algorithms, and we routinely call them “machine learning” in introductory courses!

In contrast, about 20 years ago, I saw statistics departments at a number of universities look at developments in machine learning and say, “that’s not really statistics.” This is one reason why machine learning grew much more in computer science than statistics departments. (Fortunately, since then, most statistics departments have become much more open to machine learning.)

This contrast came to mind a few months ago, as I thought about how to talk about agentic systems that use design patterns such as reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration t0 produce better results than zero-shot prompting. I had been involved in conversations about whether certain systems should count as “agents.” Rather than having to choose whether or not something is an agent in a binary way, I thought, it would be more useful to think of systems as being agent-like to different degrees. Unlike the noun “agent,” the adjective “agentic” allows us to contemplate such systems and include all of them in this growing movement.

More and more people are building systems that prompt a large language model multiple times using agent-like design patterns. But there’s a gray zone between what clearly is not an agent (prompting a model once) and what clearly is (say, an autonomous agent that, given high-level instructions, plans, uses tools, and carries out multiple, iterative steps of processing).

Rather than arguing over which work to include or exclude as being a true agent, we can acknowledge that there are different degrees to which systems can be agentic. Then we can more easily include everyone who wants to work on agentic systems. We can also encourage newcomers to start by building simple agentic workflows and iteratively make their systems more sophisticated.

In the past few weeks, I’ve noticed that, while technical people and non-technical people alike sometimes use the word “agent,” mainly only technical people use the word “agentic” (for now!). So when I see an article that talks about “agentic” workflows, I’m more likely to read it, since it’s less likely to be marketing fluff and more likely to have been written by someone who understands the technology.

Let’s keep working on agentic systems and keep welcoming anyone who wants to join our field!

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/gErMEPZC ]

input: topic: Multi-agent collaboration has emerged as a key AI agentic design pattern.
output:
Multi-agent collaboration has emerged as a key AI agentic design pattern. Given a complex task like writing software, a multi-agent approach would break down the task into subtasks to be executed by different roles -- such as a software engineer, product manager, designer, quality assurance engineer, and so on -- and have different agents accomplish different subtasks.

Different agents might be built by prompting a LLM to carry out different tasks. For example, to build a software engineer agent, we might prompt: "You are an expert in writing clear, efficient code. Write code to perform the task …". 

It might seem counterintuitive that, although we are making multiple calls to the same LLM, we apply the programming abstraction of using multiple agents. I'd like to offer a few reasons:
- It works! Many teams are getting good results with this method, and there's nothing like results! Ablation studies (for example, in the AutoGen paper cited below) show that multiple agents give superior performance to a single agent. 
- Even though some LLMs today can accept very long input contexts, their ability to truly understand long, complex inputs is mixed. An agentic workflow in which the LLM is prompted to focus on one thing at a time can give better performance. 
- It gives us a framework for breaking down complex tasks. When writing code to run on a single CPU, we often break our program up into different processes or threads. This lets us decompose a task -- like implementing a web browser -- into subtasks that are easier to code. Multi-agents roles is, similarly, a useful abstraction.

In companies, managers decide what roles to hire and then how to split complex projects into smaller tasks to assign to employees with different specialties. Using multiple agents is analogous. Each agent implements its own workflow, has its own memory (itself a rapidly evolving area in agentic technologies -- how can an agent remember enough of its past interactions to perform better on upcoming ones?), and may ask other agents for help. Agents themselves can also engage in Planning and Tool Use. While managing people is hard, it's a sufficiently familiar idea that it gives us a mental framework for how to "hire" and assign tasks to our AI agents. 

Frameworks like AutoGen, Crew AI, and LangGraph provide rich ways to build multi-agent solutions. If you're interested in playing with a fun multi-agent system, check out ChatDev, an open source implementation of a set of agents that run a virtual software company. While it may not always produce what you want, you might be amazed at how well it does! 

To learn more, I recommend: 
- Communicative Agents for Software Development, Qian et al. (2023) (the ChatDev paper)
- AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation, Wu et al. (2023) 
- MetaGPT: Meta Programming for A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework, Hong et al. (2023) 

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/g7gShRnf ]

input: topic: I'm glad the Washington Post's editorial board is pushing for governments to engage in exploring climate geoengineering.
output:
I'm glad the Washington Post's editorial board is pushing for governments to engage in exploring climate geoengineering. I believe AI climate modeling has an important role to play. 

Here's the situation as I see it:
- Earth is on track to a catastrophic 2-4 degrees Celsius of warming. The article references the UN Environment Program's estimate of 2.9 degrees on the current trajectory.
- We have a high degree of confidence that geoengineering via stratospheric aerosol injection (SAI) will significantly lower Earth's average surface temperature. The science here is really solid: Use aerosols in the atmosphere to reflect more sunlight away from earth, and we become cooler.
- So, why don't we just do it? Multiple reasons, but the biggest is that we still don't have good models for estimating how it will affect local climate and weather patterns, even though we're confident global average surface temperature will go down. 

That's why better AI climate modeling is crucial for reducing uncertainty and better understanding the impacts of various geoengineering strategies.

There're other issues to consider too, like governance, moral hazard (disincentivizing decarbonization), equity,  pollution from the aerosols, and the challenging implementation engineering. But many of these  problems become easier if we can make progress on the core problem of better understanding what impact SAI will have.

input: topic: I am a data geek and a big believer that new sensors, data and AI will revolutionize and democratize access to health and wellness! | tags: #AI #human
output:
I am a data geek and a big believer that new sensors, data and AI will revolutionize and democratize access to health and wellness! Human sensing AI is one of the investment themes of Blue Tulip Ventures. At Affectiva, I spent 20 years doing external sensing of human signals, now I’m turning that inward!

For the past couple of months I’ve been experimenting with a continuous glucose monitor (CGM). I use Dexcom 7 and the Levels app and have found the data to be fascinating. The app tracks the immediate effect foods (and activity) has on your glucose level, which is super cool. 

7 things I learnt from wearing a continuous glucose monitor: 

1- Mindful eating works: when I’m intentional about my food, starting every meal with veggies and fiber 🥦🥬🥑, then proteins 🐟 and then optionally end with a some fruit or choc (if I am craving that), my glucose doesn’t spike at all. Thank you GlucoseGoddess for that tip. 

2- Take that walk after your meals: A 30-45 minute walk after dinner really helps level my glucose level (Thank you Sophie V. Vandebroek for that tip!) 

3- Watch your snack! The way I snack is the opposite of mindful eating 😫🙈. I basically sit on my laptop and munch away! And it turns out that a spike from a handful or grapes or raspberries is equal to that of eating 3-4 squares of dark chocolate 🍫🙈!!!! (Haven’t tested what yummy Egyptian mangos will do 🥭) Any tips on what’s a great snack? 

4- What’s in your vitamins? My vitamin gummies apparently have enough sugar to spike my glucose 😔.  I am trying to transition to pills, but I must admit I haven’t been as consistent.  

5- Travel totally messes me up 😫 often, I will carry nuts and chocolate .. see my June 5th data with spikes everywhere !🙈🤦‍♀️🙈🤦‍♀️🙈🤦‍♀️🙈 .. I need to figure out how to travel w olives or something healthy like that :) airport food isn’t helpful either. Any suggestions ? 

6- Better form factor! I have to say the current sensors on the market are so unsexy and many people assumed there’s something wrong with me! I wish the sensor were more compact and discrete. I imagine that will happen as CGMs become more mainstream (in the same way that fitness and sleep trackers like WHOOP or ŌURA ring have become ubiquitous).

7- Multimodal please! How cool would it be if my CGM integrated with my whoop and other sensors like NiX. Imagine if we each had a personalized #AI health companion. This is the future and I know of at least one startup that is working on that .. 

So have you used a CGM? If yes, what have you learnt? What are some #human sensing startups that are worth looking into?

input: topic: New year. | tags: #tech
output:
New year. New adventure… In June 2021, I sold my company Affectiva to Smart Eye. 

Now, after two and a half years, it is time to explore my next adventure. Starting Feb 1st, I am transitioning out of my full time role at Smart Eye to focus on my next adventure. I will continue to support Smart Eye as an advisor, helping with the company’s strategy and AI roadmap.

I am so proud of the Smart Eye and Affectiva team and what we have accomplished together. I am grateful. 

We pioneered the #tech category of Emotion AI and brought it to market in several industries. We continue to be market leaders in automotive with more than 300 design wins from 21 car manufacturers. Our latest demos at CES showcased what the future of automotive and human-machine interfaces looks like, marrying Gen AI with Emotion AI. I am rooting for Smart Eye and look forward to seeing continued growth and success. 

So what’s next for me? It is such an exciting time in AI. The next trillion dollar companies will be AI-driven. We are seeing rapid and transformative innovation in AI. At the same time, there’s an increased need for regulation and more diversity in AI. It is an amazing time to start AI companies. Above all, it's become clear to me that the companies that will matter will have impact and be mission-driven. 

I am undecided what form my next adventure could take. Maybe I will build a new company or join / raise a fund to back amazing (female!) founders. I am still on a mission to humanize technology while advocating for diversity, ethics and impact in AI. 

I suspect that my next endeavor will be at the intersection of AI, human sensing, health & wellness, and next gen human-machine interfaces.

I am allowing myself the time to explore what I want to do next and who I want to build my next new thing with. 

My word for this year is Al Fattah (الْفَتَّاحُ), inspired from one of the 99 names of Allah and Arabic for The Opener, The Revealer, and The Granter of Success. The word’s root fa-ta-ha means to open, which in Arabic, has multiple meanings. In 2024, I look forward to new doors being opened. I look forward to co-creating new possibilities with people I respect and admire (and love working with!). I look forward to creating impact at scale. 

Finally, Fattah in Arabic also means victory and success. So here’s to lots of victory and success in 2024.

input: topic: Tool use, in which an LLM is given functions it can request to call for gathering information, taking action, or manipulating data, is a key design pattern of AI agentic workflows.
output:
Tool use, in which an LLM is given functions it can request to call for gathering information, taking action, or manipulating data, is a key design pattern of AI agentic workflows. You may be familiar with LLM-based systems that can perform web search or execute code; some large consumer-facing LLMs already incorporate these features. But tool use goes well beyond this. 

If you prompt an online LLM-based chat system, “What is the best coffee maker according to reviewers?”, it might decide to use web search to gain context. Early on, LLM developers realized that relying only on a transformer to generate output tokens is limiting, and that giving an LLM a web search tool lets it do much more. With such a tool, an LLM is either fine-tuned or prompted (perhaps with few-shot prompting) to generate a special string like {tool: web-search, query: "coffee maker reviews"} to request calling a search engine. (The exact format of the string depends on the implementation.) A post-processing step then looks for strings like these, calls the web search function with the relevant parameters when it finds one, and passes the result back to the LLM as additional input context.

Similarly, if you ask, “If I invest $100 at compound 7% interest for 12 years, what do I have at the end?”, the LLM might use a Python execution tool to compute 100 * (1+0.07)**12. The LLM might generate a string like this: {tool: python-interpreter, code: "100 * (1+0.07)**12"}.

But tool use now goes much further. Developers are using functions to perform search (web, Wikipedia, arXiv, etc.), interface with productivity tools (email, calendar, etc.), generate or interpret images, and more. We can prompt an LLM using context that gives detailed descriptions of many functions plus information about their arguments. And we’d expect the LLM to automatically choose the right function to call to do a job.

Before widespread availability of large multimodal models (LMMs) like LLaVa, GPT-4V, and Gemini, LLMs could not process images directly, so a lot of early work on tool use was carried out by the computer vision community. At that time, the only way for an LLM-based system to manipulate images was by calling a function to, say, carry out object recognition or some other function. Since then, tool use has exploded. GPT-4’s function calling, released last year, was a significant step toward general-purpose tool use. Now, more and more LLMs are being developed to similarly be facile with tool use.

To learn more, I recommend:
- Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs, Patil et al. (2023)
- MM-REACT: Prompting ChatGPT for Multimodal Reasoning and Action, Yang et al. (2023)
- Efficient Tool Use with Chain-of-Abstraction Reasoning, Gao et al. (2024)

Both Tool Use and Reflection, which I posted about last week, are design patterns that I can get to work fairly reliably, and are well worth learning about. 

[Original: https://lnkd.in/gw-EwcTB ]

input: topic: As a data scientist, your best skill is the way you think.
output:
As a data scientist, your best skill is the way you think. Not how many commands you know in R.

I spoke at DataRobot's Spring event recently and was asked how I think the role of data science will evolve.

In my opinion, there's never been a better time to be in this space. The tools will change, the scope of responsibility will expand, but I'm bullish on the role because of how data scientists are trained to think and work. 

Data scientists are experts at exploring data and thinking with precision. They start with the top-line goal in mind. A truly great data scientist brings superlative ability to translate business needs into data-driven decision-making and AI applications.

I wrote a lot more about this, including…

- Which skills are required for the future of the role
- Precision thinking and why it matters
- How business leaders will have to evolve alongside data teams
- Why the game-changing opportunities are in enterprise-scale automation

You can read the full post here: https://lnkd.in/eAUbisnM 

For the data scientists out there, I’d love to hear how you'd like the role to evolve and what changes you're already seeing in your day to day👇

input: topic: I continue to be alarmed at the progress of proposed California regulation SB 1047 and the attack it represents on open source and more broadly on AI innovation.
output:
I continue to be alarmed at the progress of proposed California regulation SB 1047 and the attack it represents on open source and more broadly on AI innovation. As I wrote previously, this proposed law makes a fundamental mistake of regulating AI technology instead of AI applications, and thus would fail to make AI meaningfully safer. I’d like to explain why the specific mechanisms of SB 1047 are so pernicious to open source.

To be clear, there are routes that regulators should pursue to improve safety. For example, I would welcome outlawing nonconsensual deepfake pornography, standardizing watermarking and fingerprinting to identify generated content, and investing more in red teaming and other safety research. Unfortunately, the proposed bill pursues a less beneficial and more harmful path.

SB 1047’s purported goal is to ensure safety of AI models. It puts in place complex reporting requirements for developers who fine-tune models or develop models that cost more than $100 million to train. It is a vague, ambiguous law that imposes significant penalties for violations, creating a huge gray zone in which developers can’t be sure how to avoid breaking the law. This will paralyze many teams.

You can read the latest draft of the law online. I’ve read through it carefully, and I find it ambiguous and very hard to follow.

Developers who try to navigate the law’s complex requirements face what feels like a huge personal risk. It requires that developers submit, under penalty of perjury, a certification of compliance with the requirements of the law. But when the requirements are complex, hard to understand, and can even shift according to the whims of an unelected body (more on this below), how do we ensure we are in compliance?

For example, the certification must include many different sections. One is an analysis of “the nature and magnitude of critical harms … the model might reasonably cause or enable.” But given that even leading AI researchers aren’t sure what harms models might cause or enable, how is a team of developers supposed to figure this out and declare — under penalty of perjury — that they meet this requirement?

Further, some developers will be required to implement “protections to prevent … misuse of, or unsafe post-training modifications of, the covered model and all covered model derivatives … that are appropriate in light of the risks associated with the covered model, including from advanced persistent threats or other sophisticated actors.” Even leading AI researchers don’t agree on how best to “protect” AI models against these supposed risks, or what would be “appropriate.” So how are developers supposed to figure out how to comply with this requirement?

[Reached LinkedIn length limit. Rest of text here: https://lnkd.in/g_NhQpni ]

input: topic: Large language models (LLMs) promise to revolutionize many aspects of the creation and dissemination of scientific knowledge; however, their use in scientific writing remains controversial, because of concerns about authorship, originality, factual inaccuracies, and “hallucinations” or confabulations. | tags: #AIinMedicine
output:
Large language models (LLMs) promise to revolutionize many aspects of the creation and dissemination of scientific knowledge; however, their use in scientific writing remains controversial, because of concerns about authorship, originality, factual inaccuracies, and “hallucinations” or confabulations. As a result, several publication venues have explicitly prohibited their use. At NEJM AI, the editors and editorial board have elected instead to allow the use of LLMs for submissions, as long as authors take complete responsibility for the content and properly acknowledge the use of LLMs. However, this policy does not allow an LLM to be listed as a coauthor. We believe that the use of LLM tools can help scientists enhance the quality of their scientific work and democratize both the creation and consumption of scientific knowledge, thereby helping us maximally enable the scientific workforce to produce robust, novel scientific findings and disseminate them broadly.

Read the full editorial by the editors and editorial board of NEJM AI on why we should allow and even encourage the use of LLMs in scientific publications: https://nejm.ai/3NmHkc9

#AIinMedicine Excited to be part of the launch of this important journal, and one that takes a rigorous yet forward-thinking view on important questions at the intersection of AI and medicine.

input: topic: When I say garbage in, you say garbage out.
output:
When I say garbage in, you say garbage out. Right?

But who in your organization is actually responsible for data quality? Whose job is it to make sure the data is well designed, intelligently collected, curated, managed, and documented?

I ask many people this question. I get different answers every time. That’s a problem!

We’ve managed to get a long way without standardizing data quality. Without making sure that what goes in is not garbage.

And it’s because we as an industry haven’t done a good job of making this role particularly appealing. You can’t blame people for wanting to do fancy math instead of looking after the inputs to someone else’s fancy math. We’ve sent decades telling aspiring data professionals that fancy math is where the glory is.

But GenAI is fueled by data. That’s what builds your moat. The teams that take the discipline of data seriously will build a much stronger foundation and get much stronger results. 

I talked more about this, and loads of other data topics, with my friend @Richie Cotton. You can watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eqQXXJR7

input: topic: Have you used quantization with an open source machine learning library, and wondered how quantization works?
output:
Have you used quantization with an open source machine learning library, and wondered how quantization works? How can you preserve model accuracy as you compress from 32 bits to 16, 8, or even 2 bits? In our new short course, Quantization in Depth, taught by Hugging Face's Marc Sun and Younes Belkada, you'll learn to implement variants of linear quantization, such as asymmetric and symmetric modes, from scratch. You'll also quantize at different granularities (per-tensor, per-channel, per-group) to maintain performance. You’ll then construct a quantizer to compress any open source deep learning model’s dense layers to 8-bit precision. Finally, you’ll practice quantizing weights into 2 bits by packing four 2-bit weights into a single 8-bit integer.

If you've ever run a large open source model on your laptop, you've likely benefited from someone's work in quantization. Come learn how this key technique works under the hood!

input: topic: The Great Equalizer: Does AI level the playing field?
output:
The Great Equalizer: Does AI level the playing field?

A recent newsletter I read argued that AI pulls bottom performers closer to the top, citing 3 studies:
Out of 5,000 customer support agents, junior workers were 35% faster than usual, but top performers saw little improvement. 
The bottom half of BCG consultants saw a 43% boost in work quality using ChatGPT-4, compared to just 17% for the top performers.
A law school study discovered that “the lowest-skilled participants saw the largest improvements” in performance.

And, of course, yours truly can appear to be a half-decent illustrator thanks to Midjourney and the artists whose work was used to train it.

An alternative UX-based take:
“Just because early results for AI suggest that only lower performing people benefit does not mean that this is the only possible pattern. It may be that the reason only lower performers gain from AI currently is because the current AI systems are not good enough to help top performers.” --Ethan Mollick

If AI is the great equalizer, why would it make bad programmers worse at their jobs? What gives?

Because these studies are looking at marginal individual productivity rather than marginal enterprise productivity. Yes, it's possible to write code faster but it's also possible to submit code that's not fully vetted faster... and it's also possible to submit code that solves the wrong problem faster (and to also to write code that creates problems you didn't have before). So before you get caught up in the excitement of individual productivity boosters, do remember that these effects aren't taking the broader context into account. Most GenAI tools are still built with individual UX in mind and not organization-wide design, so do remember to take a pause if you're tempted to draw general conclusions about the relationship between skill/IQ and performance.

What are your thoughts?

input: topic: The most important news in AI this week that you haven’t heard about yet.
output:
The most important news in AI this week that you haven’t heard about yet.

And no it's not about Gemini.

Eric Ries (creator of The Lean Startup) and Jeremy Howard (Co-founder of Kaggle, fast.ai) are teaming up to create a new kind of AI lab.

It's called Answer.AI.

Answer.AI is a new lab that isn’t aiming to build new models.

But instead trying to figure out practical and valuable uses for existing ones.

Their mission is to do fundamental research for organizations to help them unlock AI-enabled use cases.

“At Answer.AI we are not working on building AGI. Instead, our interest is in effectively using the models that already exist. Figuring out what practically useful applications can be built on top of the foundation models that already exist is a huge undertaking, and I believe it is receiving insufficient attention.”

This is what I’m excited about most: Their focus on application.

My view is that underneath all of the hype and noise around AI today (scroll on LinkedIn for 5 minutes and you’ll see what I mean), there is real business value waiting to be discovered. Actually no - there is real human value to be discovered.

Like electricity, or a screwdriver, AI is just another tool at our disposal.

Only through the intentional application of those tools does real value begin to emerge.

input: topic: Meet Sohu, the fastest AI chip of all time. | tags: #AI
output:
Meet Sohu, the fastest AI chip of all time.

With over 500,000 tokens per second in Llama 70B throughput, Sohu lets you build products impossible on GPUs.

Sohu is the world’s first specialized chip (ASIC) for transformers (the “T” in ChatGPT). By burning the transformer architecture into our chip, we can’t run most traditional AI models. But for generative AI models, like ChatGPT (text), SD3 (images), and Sora (video), Sohu has unparalleled performance.

One Sohu server runs over 500,000 Llama 70B tokens per second: >20x more than an H100 server (23,000 tokens/sec), and >10x more than a B200 server.

We recently raised $120M from Primary Venture Partners and Positive Sum, with participation from Two Sigma Ventures, Skybox Datacenters, Hummingbird Ventures, Oceans, Fundomo, Velvet Sea Ventures, Fontinalis Partners, Galaxy, Earthshot Ventures, Max Ventures and Lightscape Partners.

We’re grateful for the support of industry leaders, including Peter Thiel, David Siegel, Thomas Dohmke, Jason Warner, Amjad Masad, Kyle Vogt, Stanley Freeman Druckenmiller, and many more.

We’re on track for one of the fastest chip launches in history:
- Top hardware engineers and AI researchers have left every major AI chip project to join us.
- We’ve partnered directly with TSMC on their 4nm process. We’ve secured HBM and server supply from top vendors and can quickly ramp our first year of production.
- Our early customers have reserved tens of millions of dollars of our hardware.

As we hit the limits of speed, cost, and scale on GPUs, specialized chips are inevitable. If you want to change the future of AI compute, please join us at www.etched.com/careers.

(Benchmarks are from running in FP8 without sparsity at 8x model parallelism with 2048 input/128 output lengths. 8xH100s figures are from TensorRT-LLM 0.10.08 (latest version), and 8xB200 figures are estimated. This is the same benchmark NVIDIA and AMD use.) The AI chip landscape, currently dominated by NVIDIA is SO ripe for disruption. Congratulations Etched on your $120M Series A round. I am so proud to be an investor. 

Training AI models today cost billions of dollars (not to mention that training these models consumes the power equivalent of what a country like Costa Rica consumes in a whole year!). At the current pace, our hardware, our power grids, and pocketbooks can’t keep up. 

Etched is changing this. In 2022, they made a bet that transformers (the “T” in ChatGPT) would take over the world. The team made that bet even before OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public - how cool! Today, every state-of-the-art AI model is a transformer: ChatGPT, Sora, Gemini, Stable Diffusion 3, and more. 

The team Etched spent the past two years building Sohu, the world’s first specialized chip (ASIC) for transformers. Sohu is an order of magnitude faster and cheaper than NVIDIA’s next generation of Blackwell (GB200) GPUs when running text, image, and video transformers.

Etched is making one the biggest bets in #AI right now and they are on track for one of the fastest chip launches in history. If they pull this off, every AI product will run on their chips. Excited to be on this journey with the team. 

Gavin Uberti Robert W.

input: topic: I think AI agentic machine translation has huge potential for improving over traditional neural machine translation, and am releasing as open-source a demonstration I'd been playing with as a fun weekend project.
output:
I think AI agentic machine translation has huge potential for improving over traditional neural machine translation, and am releasing as open-source a demonstration I'd been playing with as a fun weekend project.

Using an agentic workflow, this demonstration (i) Prompts an LLM to translate from one language to another, (ii) Reflects on the translation to come up with constructive suggestions, (iii) Uses the suggestions to refine the translation. In our limited testing, this is sometimes competitive with, and sometimes worse than, leading commercial providers.

But it gives a highly steerable translation system where by simply changing the prompt, you can specify the tone (formal/informal), regional variation (do you want Spanish as spoken in Spain or as spoken in Latin America?), and ensure consistent translation of terms (by providing a glossary).

This is not mature software. But I hope the open-source community can make agentic translation work much better. Given how a simple reflection workflow already gives decent results, I think there's significant headroom to make agentic translation much better.

Releasing an early software prototype like this is something new I decided to try to see if it is helpful to the developer community. I'd love any feedback on this.

Thanks to Joaquin Dominguez, Nedelina Teneva, PhD and John Santerre PhD for help with this.

input: topic: There's a viral video going around from an MIT-trained neurosurgeon who quit his job at 38 to move to the woods.
output:
There's a viral video going around from an MIT-trained neurosurgeon who quit his job at 38 to move to the woods. 

Since I’m heading into the woods myself today to take a small break from the breakneck pace of building a business and make sure the version of me I share with the world is balanced and cheerful, I thought I’d make a little incision into this neurosurgeon’s decision to finally quit.

Everyone in his life, of course, told him to stay. Look at the facts, they said: "You have 15 years of training, extraordinary pay, life-saving skills, and the prestige of being a brain surgeon. You have no other skills, your wife isn't working, and you have no plan to make money. You cannot quit.”

Most people would expect me to pick a side, either expressing alarm at his quitting his job without a plan or applauding him for being brave enough to quit (with full support from his wife) a job in which he felt sad, frustrated, angry, and stuck. The trouble is that I’d be teaching a poor lesson either way, since I’d be encouraging you to buy into the idea that there are only ever two options on the table. 

In reality, there are always many more possibilities than we realize. We don’t have to get to a breaking point before we pivot and start steering our lives in the direction we’d like to go in.

Don’t let things get so bad that you take the decision to quit out of your own hands. If you don’t like your job, the way to quit isn’t to wait until burnout. That reminds me of using a wall as a means of stopping your car. Don’t do that… I’d recommend using the brakes and the steering wheel to go where you need to.
I’ll link the video in the blog post below, but not as inspiration to quit a bad situation immediately.

I’ve made major pivots many times in my life, but I’ve never done it without setting up some decent options first. To do this, I’ve made sure never to let things get to a place where they’re truly on fire. Yes, if you single-mindedly pursue a goal without checking in with yourself about what makes you happy and if you let things get as bad as this fellow did (and I do believe him when he says they’re as bad for him as they are) then quitting the way he did is the right move.

But that's not the best approach. I'm convinced that one key to a great life is regular check-ins with yourself. In fact, I'd say you should always be quitting (just a little bit). Before you react, let me explain what I mean here:

input: topic: (Don't) Ask Astro?
output:
(Don't) Ask Astro? You might want to know what security pests this little a16z chatbot is harboring before you use it. Let's talk about the state of AI security!

Background: Ask Astro is an astronomy chatbot modeled on reference LLM app architecture from Andreessen Horowitz (that hallowed VC firm whose name you're likely to have seen abbrev. a16z), so you'd think it would be secure, right? After all, it's a fairly straightforward example of a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) application.

Because it comes from a16z, this architecture is an influential guide that many developers reference in the hopes of building scalable and robust AI solutions within the tech industry... and unfortunately it's crawling with badness.

A team at cybersecurity firm Trail of Bits, led by my friend Dan Guido, did a comprehensive security audit of Ask Astro and discovered several substantial "hybrid ML security" issues in it that blur the line between machine learning and systems security. These include split-view data poisoning, prompt injection, and GraphQL injection... these are all ways that attackers can manipulate the chatbot’s responses by exploiting weaknesses in how data is handled.

Glossary:
🐞 Split-view data poisoning occurs when an attacker changes online data after it's been indexed by the system, leading to discrepancies between what's stored and what's currently true.
🐞 Prompt injection involves inserting misleading cues to make the AI generate specific, often incorrect, outputs.
🐞 GraphQL injection exposes the chatbot to potential data breaches by allowing attackers to craft malicious queries that manipulate the database interactions, potentially leaking or corrupting data.

Here's a link to the blog post, which you should definitely share with whoever on your team is responsible for AI security: 

https://lnkd.in/eVq6TDw4

(Not kidding, this is useful to them. The security audit also provides best practices that can benefit anyone developing similar technologies. Go on, send it, I'll wait. Also, if you're using AI at work and *no one* is responsible for AI security, well, uh-oh. Share this with whoever's in charge, then.)

Now, for the rest of us, what does it mean?

People seem to be taking for granted that AI security is in a better place than  it actually is. We're complacent. See that picture of the baby car seat from the 1950s? AI today looks to AI security experts like that baby car seat looks to you. What could possibly go wrong?

No one is saying that we should all stop driving, but pretty please take the idea of seat belts and safety-oriented design seriously. Same goes for AI. But right now we're all driving without seatbelts.

Drop your questions in the comments, since the folks from Trail of Bits will be watching this space today to answer anything you want to know about AI security.

https://lnkd.in/eVq6TDw4

output:
In the summer of 2021, OpenAI quietly shuttered its robotics team, announcing that progress was being stifled by a lack of data necessary to train robots in how to move and reason using artificial intelligence. 

Now three of OpenAI’s early research scientists say the startup they spun off in 2017, called Covariant, has solved that problem and unveiled a system that combines the reasoning skills of large language models with the physical dexterity of an advanced robot.

The new model, called RFM-1, was trained on years of data collected from Covariant’s small fleet of item-picking robots that customers use in warehouses around the world, as well as words and videos from the internet. In the coming months, the model will be released to Covariant customers. The company hopes the system will become more capable and efficient as it’s deployed in the real world.

input: topic: On this week’s Unsupervised Learning, Jordan Segall and I dive into the forefront of AI innovation with Eric Ries and Jeremy Howard.
output:
On this week’s Unsupervised Learning, Jordan Segall and I dive into the forefront of AI innovation with Eric Ries and Jeremy Howard. Eric and Jeremy have had incredible careers including Eric pioneering the Lean Startup movement and Jeremy co-founding fast.ai. They recently founded Answer.ai, an R&D lab aiming to the Bell Labs for AI. In this episode, they share insights into the evolving landscape of AI agents, the practical applications of their research, and their unique approach. Some highlights from our conversation: 

Too Many Splashy Demos

Eric draws a parallel between the development of electricity and the current state of AI to emphasize the necessity for practical applications over “splashy demos”. He points out that, like early electrical innovations, the potential uses of AI are clear but transitioning from experimental prototypes to reliable, widely deployed technology requires extensive and often painstaking development. Eric criticizes the focus on flashy AI demos that lack substantive progress toward real-world usability and deployment. He advocates for an Edison-like focus on resolving practical issues, improving the ergonomics, deployability, and cost-efficiency of AI technologies to truly transform them into usable products. This approach, he argues, is crucial for AI to move beyond the lab and into everyday applications.

Jeremy’s Concern with the latest California AI legislation 

Jeremy highlighted concerns about a proposed Californian law (SB 1047) intended to ensure AI safety. Despite its good intentions, he argues that the part of the law that attempts to ensure the safety of AI models could unintentionally decrease safety by restricting access to AI models. He explains that AI models, like neutral tools such as pens or the internet, can be used for both beneficial and harmful purposes. This fundamental characteristic makes it impossible to guarantee a model's safety completely, as its use and outcomes depend significantly on how it is applied by end-users.

Jeremy stressed that even with rigorous testing, a model that appears safe in controlled conditions might be repurposed to do harm, similar to how innocuous technology can be adapted for negative uses. He argued that the proposed law could lead to restrictive practices that prevent the widespread beneficial use of AI, creating an environment where AI technology is hoarded by a few large players, reducing transparency and stifling innovation in fields where AI could have profoundly positive impacts.

When Agents Make Sense 

Agents excel in tasks that involve recombining elements seen in their training data, such as managing emails, adding CSS to HTML, or debugging Python code. However, they struggle with tasks requiring novel planning or algorithm creation not represented in the training data. 

So much more packed in this episode - enjoy!

input: topic: The great irony of being an AI advisor is that many enterprise leaders who come to me don’t need AI at all.
output:
The great irony of being an AI advisor is that many enterprise leaders who come to me don’t need AI at all.

Like most things in life, the best approach in enterprise is to start not with the technology, but with the business problem you’re trying to solve.

AI may be the solution you need. But it should be what you try after traditional programming fails.* When you have something to automate, but you aren’t able to do it with your existing bag of tricks. When the need is so critical that you’re willing to add complexity and the reduction of control that comes with it.

*This doesn't mean traditional programming has to have failed on your watch. There are quite a few problems that ~others~ have tried and failed to solve without AI... in which case you can jump straight to AI. The kiss of death for enterprise value, though, is throwing AI at poorly defined problems or problems better suited to non-AI solutions that no one bothered to try. Always start with the business problem, no matter what the sales reps say. 

(For those keeping score at home, I do indeed believe this is the best image AI has ever generated for me, it gets funnier the longer I look at it.)

input: topic: When we get to AGI, it will have come slowly, not overnight.
output:
When we get to AGI, it will have come slowly, not overnight. 

A NeurIPS Outstanding Paper award recipient, Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage? (by Rylan Schaeffer, Brando Miranda and Sanmi Koyejo) studies emergent properties of LLMs, and concludes: 
"... emergent abilities appear due the researcher’s choice of metric rather than due to fundamental changes in model behavior with scale. Specifically, nonlinear or discontinuous metrics produce apparent emergent abilities, whereas linear or continuous metrics produce smooth, continuous, predictable changes in model performance." 

Public perception goes through discontinuities when lots of people suddenly become aware of a technology -- maybe one that's been developing for a long time --  leading to a surprise. But growth in AI capabilities is more continuous than one might think. 

That's why I expect the path to AGI to be one involving numerous steps forward, leading to step-by-step improvements in how intelligent our systems are.

input: topic: Reflecting on ChatGPT’s one year anniversary and looking ahead to what’s next in #GenerativeAi in an interview with Reid Hoffman at Fortune BrainstormAI 2023. | tags: #GenerativeAi #FortuneAI
output:
Reflecting on ChatGPT’s one year anniversary and looking ahead to what’s next in #GenerativeAi in an interview with Reid Hoffman at Fortune BrainstormAI 2023. And what a conversation that was! 

ChatGPT launched on Nov. 30, 2022, disrupting industries, internet search and, according to some, human intelligence itself. A year later, the success of ChatGPT has spawned numerous, well-funded rivals (even the French are in this race :)), and a fierce debate between techno-optimists and so-called "doomers". 

Of course I had to start by asking Reid about the recent drama at OpenAI! Reid was on OpenAI’s board until last March when he stepped down to avoid conflict of interest with his other investments. I was particularly intrigued by Reid’s POV that OpenAI’s governance structure - a not-for-profit that oversees a commercial org - is perfectly feasible and there’s precedent for it (e.g., Mozilla) … as long as there’s alignment of values and direction. 

We also reflected on some of the positive and perhaps surprising use-cases of ChatGPT over the past year: how Gen Z’s use ChatGPT and GenAI to explore special interests, synthesize information and for creative self-expression.

Reid dreams big: every person will have access to a personal AI assistant. Inflection.ai, the company he co-founded with Mustafa Suleyman is building Pi (Personal Intelligence), a kind and empathetic AI bot that can be a friend, coach, thought partner and helps you be the best version of yourself. LOVE THAT! 

AI has the potential to elevate humanity! But of course, there are risks. We discussed the role of regulation including Biden’s Executive Order and the EU AI act. One area of concern is how AI hallucinations and deep fakes will lead to misinformation in the upcoming election cycle in the US. 

Building on my conversation with Reid, I then moderated a discussion on how “Chatbots are the Talk on AI Innovation” with Eugenia Kuyda, Founder & CEO at Replika, Stefanos Loukakos, CEO at Connectly.ai and Vipul Vyas, Senior Vice President at Persado.

With the help of companies like Connectly and Persado, retailers are using conversational bots to personalize sales outreach and interactions, leveraging emotional messages to evoke action. Replika is on a mission to combat loneliness. But do chatbots that offer friendship and intimacy help us feel less lonely or … more lonely? 

Finally, with Google’s Gemini recent launch, the future will bring multi-model AI bots that interact through text, voice and video. 

Ps. Reid's impromptu book is a must-read: https://lnkd.in/eqWWK6ru

Jeremy Kahn Verne Kopytoff Michal Lev-Ram 

#FortuneAI

input: topic: 🤖 📈 The AI hardware industry has thrived with the introduction of open benchmarks of AI chips, benchmarks like MLPerf by MLCommons.
output:
🤖 📈 The AI hardware industry has thrived with the introduction of open benchmarks of AI chips, benchmarks like MLPerf by MLCommons. 🏥 🚀 Medical AI could benefit from open benchmarks too: very happy to share MedPerf - a framework and call for participation based on federated evaluation, with contribution from academia and industry, supported by MLCommons.

🎯 We pressure-tested MedPerf open benchmark framework by supporting the largest real-world federated study to date (FeTS - that was benchmarking 41 medical AI models across 32 treatment and research sites in 6 continents 🌎) as well as on 🔓 public and 🔒 private data through medical research efforts across Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, University of Pennsylvania, Penn Medicine, University of Pennsylvania Health System, University of Strasbourg, Institute of Image-Guided Surgery (IHU Strasbourg), Fondazione Policlinico Universitario Agostino Gemelli IRCCS.

🌀 MedPerf is nuanced to revolve around the concept of a human committee, which is accountable for the medical AI task and coordinates benchmark stakeholders (e.g., patients, clinicians, those who have the data for the AI to use, researchers who write the AI models, etc.).

🌀 MedPerf benchmarks are designed to be agnostic of the input data type - radiology studies, structured EHR data, pathology whole-slide images, genomics files, surgery videos, multi-modal input, you name it.

🌀 MedPerf focuses on federated evaluation, i.e. inference only and no training, so that you can assess how well your AI models generalize when deployed in other hospitals or on populations that are different than those you trained on. 

📜 Paper: https://lnkd.in/eeRRqx_m
📣 Call for participation: https://lnkd.in/eYp_afT6

🙏 Huge thanks to all 70+ people, 20+ universities, 9 hospitals, and 20+ companies that participated in the design, implementation, and validation of MedPerf: Alexandros Karargyris Renato Umeton, Ph.D. (Hiring) Micah Sheller Alejandro Aristizábal Johnu George Anna Wuest Sarthak Pati Hasan Kassem Alexander Chowdhury Junyi Guo Sahil Nalawade Jacob Rosenthal Debojyoti Dutta Satyananda Kashyap Nicholas Lane Indranil Mallick Nikola Nikolov Gennady Pekhimenko Vijay Janapa Reddi Thomas Wolf Geralyn Miller Prashant Shah Daguang Xu David Talby Jeremy Howard Luigi Marchionni Massimo Loda Jason Johnson Spyridon Bakas Peter Mattson and many others joining from https://lnkd.in/eXnB3qvH 🙏

🏗 We still have a lot of work ahead in medical AI. Having the medical AI models delivered to the hospitals to assess how well the AI generalizes as part of an open benchmark, without sharing patient data, feels like a step in the right direction toward better and more inclusive AI-enabled medicine.

A riddle! Name something both helped and hurt by AI.

.
.
.
.
.
.
.

There are probably many answers to that, but the right answer today is 🔎 FRAUD 🔍.

Fraudsters are creating more sophisticated phishing scams by using GenAI to create fake voices, images, videos and paper trails. But the good guys are starting to wield AI as well, particularly LLMs... which can be very helpful for:

- Analyzing transaction data to spot anomalous patterns such as unusually large transfers or frequent small transactions.
- Detecting phishing attempts and fraudulent communication by analyzing the language and structure of emails.
- Understanding typical customer behavior and flagging deviations that indicate account takeover or fraud attempts.
- Evaluating the authenticity of documents like invoices, receipts, and contracts by comparing them against known legitimate documents.

output:
Outside success like wealth and fame, does not always lead to inner joy and peace, unless it is aligned with your Dharma! Suneel Gupta tackles this head on in his MUST READ book “Everyday Dharma”. 

Your Dharma is your essence or inner calling. We all feel best when there is alignment of that essence and what we do - we feel confident, creative, and caring, with a sense of purpose that shines through our life and work. The good news is Dharma is not something you have to go out and find - it's already inside you, but has been buried under layers of expectations, judgments, and the realities of daily life. Everyday Dharma offers practical exercises that help you uncover your dharma. I went all out and created a fun mind map exploring my dharma :) 

My essence is to spark joy thru human connection and possibility. If you know me, you will know that I love meeting new people, hearing their stories, inspiring, opening doors and imagineering possibility. It gives me great pleasure, even when there is nothing in it for me. The book showed me there are many ways to manifest my dharma - from investing in founders of AI startups, especially women, to hosting events like the Affectiva Emotion AI Summit and Fortune  Brainstorm AI conferences, where cross pollination of ideas and amazing connections happen. But I guess I could also be a matchmaker LOL! 

What’s YOUR dharma? And how do you express it in the world?

input: topic: Approaching the AI control system question from the perspective of fear is clickbait.
output:
Approaching the AI control system question from the perspective of fear is clickbait. Talking about "smarter than us" without defining "smarter" is clickbait. Machines have better memory than us and can operate at mind-boggling scale. Already. Today.

And technology is getting more complex rapidly.

So how about we approach the question of control from the perspective of how to make complex tech useful and who should be qualified to steer it?

We need systems designed to help steer complex technology (AI and human-made) in a way that is useful, safe, and effective... and doesn't require the people leading and executing projects to have graduate degrees in AI or AI-adjacent fields. 

So when I say control systems, here's what I mean: a way to effectively steer complex things in a world that's getting increasingly complex. The fact that they'd help you with AGI (one day) is a byproduct. There are plenty of other things that need steering and we seem to have collectively forgotten there's no steering wheel.

Shouldn't we be ashamed of building systems that are far reaching in nature (already) without also building the tools that allow them to be shaped and steered by the people most qualified to lead and the domain experts most qualified to understand the nuances of the context we're launching them into? Applied AI leadership shouldn't require a graduate degree in an AI-adjacent field. Surely that's a tipoff that our control tools aren't good enough yet. Surely we should be embarrassed that we haven't annihilated the boundary between the technical and the non-technical yet. We clearly need better tools to form a solid control system. A system that guarantees tech offers what those in charge actually wanted, not what they said they wanted, while also representing the laws and needs of society.

And I don't see a control system that allows a standard senior executive (non-technical, but with domain expertise) to participate well in AI design, development, and testing. To get their perspective on their organization's needs, preferences, priorities, and risk tolerances represented and executed on effectively. Frankly, I don't see many solutions for even testing complex systems, so no wonder all these attempts at "enterprise scale GenAI without a human in the loop" keep dying not with a bang but with a whimper. How are we going to trust it with anything important if we can't test it?

I believe the path to leadership in tomorrow's world shouldn't require a STEM background. It should require a leadership background and the right tools for those leaders to build/buy the tech they need and control it so it works as *they* need it to. I call that a control system. And we don't have one yet.

input: topic: Huge announcement from Meta.
output:
Huge announcement from Meta. Welcome Llama 3.1! This is all you need to know about it:

The new models:
- The Meta Llama 3.1 family of multilingual large language models (LLMs) is a collection of pre-trained and instruction-tuned generative models in 8B, 70B, and 405B sizes (text in/text out). 

- All models support long context length (128k) and are optimized for inference with support for grouped query attention (GQA).

- Optimized for multilingual dialogue use cases and outperform many of the available open source chat models on common industry benchmarks.

- Llama 3.1 is an auto-regressive language model with an optimized transformer architecture, using SFT and RLHF for alignment. Its core LLM architecture is the same dense structure as Llama 3 for text input and output.

- Tool use, Llama 3.1 Instruct Model (Text) is fine-tuned for tool use, enabling it to generate tool calls for search, image generation, code execution, and mathematical reasoning, and also supports zero-shot tool use.

Llama System Safety:
- Models & Inference Guardrails: Llama-Guard-3 8B (multilingual text in)
- Prompt Guard (direct and indirect prompt injection filtering)
- Cybersecurity: CyberSec Eval 3, a new benchmark for assessing LLM security risks, introducing tests for prompt injection and code interpreter abuse, revealing unresolved conditioning issues, proposing the False Refusal Rate (FRR) to measure safety-utility tradeoffs, and providing open-source code for further evaluations.

Of course, the IBM team is working hard and collaborating with Meta to bring you this fantastic update on watsonx.ai, making it available with the following features:
- Real-time inference
- Batch inference
- RAG
- Model Evaluation
- Safety Guardrails
- Synthetic Data Generation

We are pleased to share the first long-form look into one part of insitro’s biology discovery platform, CellPaint-POSH, a machine learning-enabled high-content pooled optical screening system we’ve worked to build and productionize over the past four years. Our new paper, “A Pooled Cell Painting CRISPR Screening Platform Enables de novo Inference of Gene Function by Self-supervised Deep Learning,” shows that pooled genetic screening with high-content cellular imaging can enable better understanding of gene function to discover novel targets and effective therapies.

Congratulations to the cross-functional insitro team – including Srinivasan Sivanandan, Bobby Leitmann, Daphne Koller, Eilon Sharon, Ajamete Kaykas, Max Salick, Ci Chu and colleagues – for this new research, submitted for peer review. Read our preprint: https://lnkd.in/gXRauGPY

input: topic: Bacterial resistance to existing antibiotics is an urgent threat.
output:
Bacterial resistance to existing antibiotics is an urgent threat. Developing new drugs is an expensive, time-consuming, and failure-prone process, with only a small percentage of potential drug candidates making it from discovery through clinical testing to regulatory approval. Antibiotic research has been particularly neglected (compared to other types of drugs) due to unfavorable financial incentives.

Some AI-assisted approaches to antibiotic discovery have built on protein folding prediction.  Other work has created deep learning models directly for the underlying problem, to predict which drugs will have antibacterial activity.  This approach has been used to discover new antibiotics which are structurally distinct from existing classes.

Antibiotics discovered through deep learning-assisted processes is still in preliminary stages. In other fields, machine learning models have sometimes performed well in testing but failed to work as expected when deployed in the real world...

On-device (edge) AI is an important technology trend that's enabling new low latency, privacy-preserving applications. In this course, you'll deploy a real-time image segmentation model on-device, and through this learn key steps for on-device deployment: Neural Network graph capture, on-device compilation, hardware acceleration, and validating on-device numerical correctness. input: topic: So many of you have asked me what I am up to for my next adventure. | tags: #AI #HumanFirst
output:
So many of you have asked me what I am up to for my next adventure. I am excited to share that Gabi Zijderveld and I are starting a venture firm to invest in AI startups. It’s early days - we are just a few months in, so it will be some time before we deploy capital, but we are moving fast.

We are starting Blue Tulip Ventures, a venture firm that will invest in early stage startups building Human Centric AI – AI that is good for you, good for the planet and is ethical.

Some of you might not know that I have a venture track record. A couple of years ago, I raised a fund with Rob May in just 10 months, and we invested in 40 early stage AI startups. 

I recently joined Affectiva’s former investors BY Venture Partners as an AI partner, and we are now exploring ways Blue Tulip can collaborate with BY and complement their new fund’s core strategy. 

Additionally, I am a Venture Partner at BOLD Capital Partners, an LP at Underscore VC and made 20+ angel investments. There is something special about early stage startups - the passion and conviction is contagious. I love supporting founders turning their ideas into reality and want to keep doing more of that :) 

I am an entrepreneur at heart and have been thinking about starting my own venture firm for a while. And who better to partner with on this than Gabi! Gabi and I have worked together for 10 years at Affectiva. 

We had an amazing collaboration and are thought partners, so much so that we often complete each other’s sentences haha. 

We are very aligned on what we want to build:

• We believe AI will be transformative and will offer massive economic opportunity, but it has to be done right. 

• We believe AI needs to be in service of humanity, supporting us to be healthier, happier, more productive and connected … enhancing what it means to be human in the age of AI.  

• We believe AI can help protect our planet, through food innovation and sustainable living, address the impact of climate change and power scientific discovery.

• We believe AI needs to be developed and used ethically, ensuring it does not cause harm to humans or the planet, and that it is transparent and safe with proper governance.

• We have conviction that the answer is human-centric AI. AI that puts people and the planet we live on first. We want to invest in and build diverse teams and ideas, as we fundamentally believe that diversity fosters innovation and mitigates bias and injustice.

So why the Blue Tulip?

Achieving almost unicorn status, the extraordinary blue tulip is rare and elusive in nature. Its beauty signifies creativity and uniqueness. Gifting a blue tulip expresses that the recipient is unique. At Blue Tulip Ventures, we invest in hard-to-find early-stage startups that build human-centric #AI, and we celebrate them with the blue tulip because they are unlike anyone else and inspire us.

We know we have so many supporters among you who will join us on this journey. 

Stay tuned, more to come.

#HumanFirst

input: topic: Covariant's RFM-1 (Robotics Foundation Model Generation 1) is a multimodal any-to-any sequence model.
output: Covariant's RFM-1 (Robotics Foundation Model Generation 1) is a multimodal any-to-any sequence model. RFM-1 tokenizes 5 modalities: video, keyframes, text, sensory readings, robot actions.  Any-to-any means RFM-1 can handle any subset on the input and any subset on the output.  For example RFM-1 can power a robot to look at the current situation and generate the right actions.  Or it can look at the current situation and consider a sequence of actions and generate a video prediction of what'll happen next.  And it can even be instructed and conversed with in plain English!

input: topic: Every time some genius decides to apply AI where it doesn’t belong, the world collectively rolls its eyes and puts another ballot in the AI-Is-A-Fad box. | tags: #AI #usecases
output:
Every time some genius decides to apply AI where it doesn’t belong, the world collectively rolls its eyes and puts another ballot in the AI-Is-A-Fad box. Which is why it's important to ask yourself: do you actually need AI to solve your problem?

AI excels when the task is too complex for traditional programming. For example, teaching a computer to generate impressive video footage -- anyone else playing with the latest drop from Runway? -- would be nearly impossible to do by writing out explicit instructions. So we feed the computer examples (data!) and let it learn that way instead.

But for many problems - even large problems, even enterprise-scale problems - we'll get more reliable, accurate results by expressing our wishes to the machines in the most precise way possible. So how do you select the right approach?

I'm excited to join CodeSignal for a webinar to answer questions just like this. We'll cover #AI trends, impacts, and how to identify AI #usecases that will actually drive results.

You can join us live next week on Wednesday, July 10 at 12:30 ET. I hope to see you there!

input: topic: A good way to get started in AI is to start with coursework, which gives a systematic way to gain knowledge, and then to work on projects.
output:
A good way to get started in AI is to start with coursework, which gives a systematic way to gain knowledge, and then to work on projects. For many who hear this advice, “projects” may evoke a significant undertaking that delivers value to users. But I encourage you to set a lower bar and relish small, weekend tinkering projects that let you learn, even if they don’t result in a meaningful deliverable. 

Recently, my son and daughter (ages 3 and 5) were building Lego vehicles. They built a beautiful ice-cream truck as well as a . . . umm . . . colorful and asymmetric dinosaur car, shown in the picture. While most observers would judge the ice-cream truck as the superior creation, my kids built it by following Lego’s instructions, and it is likely identical to thousands of ice-cream trucks built by others. In contrast, building the dinosaur car required creativity and novel thinking. The exercise helped them hone their ability to pick and assemble Lego building blocks.

There is, of course, room for both mimicking others’ designs (with permission) and coming up with your own. As a parent, I try to celebrate both. (To be honest, I celebrated the dinosaur car more.) When learning to build Lego, it’s helpful to start by following a template. But eventually, building your own unique projects enriches your skills. 

As a developer, too, I try to celebrate unique creations. Yes, it is nice to have beautiful software, and the impact of the output does matter. But good software is often written by people who spend many hours tinkering and building things. By building unique projects, you master key software building blocks. Then, using those blocks, you can go on to build bigger projects.

I routinely tinker with building AI applications, and a lot of my tinkering doesn’t result in anything useful. My latest example: I built a Streamlit app that would authenticate to Google docs, read the text in a doc, use a large language model to edit my text, and write the result back into the doc. I didn’t find it useful in the end because of friction in the user interface, and I’m sure a commercial provider will soon, if they haven’t already, build a better product than I was able to throw together in a couple of hours on a weekend. But such tinkering helps me hone my intuition and master software components (I now know how to programmatically interface with Google docs) that might be useful in future projects.  

If you have an idea for a project, I encourage you to build it! Often, working on a project will also help you decide what additional skills to learn, perhaps through coursework. To sustain momentum, it helps to find friends with whom to talk about ideas and celebrate projects — large or small. 

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/gjx6FNdu ]
